Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All cross-generation validation uses a single held-out target GPU (GB200) trained from three to five source GPUs; the claimed 1.8x improvement over NeuSight is one data point in generation-extrapolation space, not a distribution over multiple unseen-architecture scenarios, so the generality of the 12.1% MAPE figure to, e.g., a hypothetical next-generation GPU beyond Blackwell is unverified. - No ablation isolates the contribution of the wave-quantization-in-roofline design choice (Section 5.4) versus folding that term into eta; the paper asserts this choice keeps the residual smooth but does not show a controlled comparison. - The paper does not report code or data release, which limits independent verification of the 12.1%/3.8% MAPE headline numbers and the vLLM-scheduling fidelity claim (Spearman 0.985). - No error bars or seed variation are given anywhere, so the reader cannot judge whether, e.g., the 3.0%/1.0% p50 throughput-error gap between KernelSight-LM and AIConfigurator (Section 7.3) is a stable effect or within measurement noise. - Table 5 lists an RTX 5090 and GB10 as edge accelerators profiled but explicitly excluded from the cross-generation ladder "as noise"; this asymmetric curation (keeping only datacenter-class GPUs in the transfer study) is a reasonable methodological choice but is not stress-tested against consumer-class targets, where the efficiency-transfer problem could plausibly be worse. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or selecting a hardware-capacity-planning tool for LLM serving gains a working template for combining roofline-based cross-device kernel prediction with full serving-dynamics simulation, and the paper's own limitations analysis (Appendix B) is itself a useful, actionable characterization of exactly which kernel families to trust with zero-shot cross-generation prediction and which require at least one on-device measurement.
Recommended 2606.28565 PDF
#17 — Innovative (67)

Geometry-Aware Online Scheduling for LLM Serving: From Theoretical Bound to System Practice

L Kong, Q Qi, Y Ye, Z Zhou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Smallest Volume First (SVF), an online LLM-serving scheduler that ranks requests by the 2D integral of prompt-plus-decode memory footprint over time rather than by predicted job length alone, plus a 1-bit variant (1-bit SVF) that needs only a binary short/long classification. - Why it's important: KV-cache memory, not raw compute, is the binding constraint in continuous-batching LLM inference, and the dominant heuristic (Shortest Job First, SJF) has a provably unbounded worst-case competitive ratio under this constraint. - How it does it: SVF sorts requests by volume v_i = s_i·o_i + (o_i^2+o_i)/2 (prompt length times output length plus the token-by-token quadratic growth term) and admits greedily in ascending volume order, with a volume-certificate proof technique replacing prior time-window discretization arguments. - The good: The paper tightens the worst-case competitive ratio for burst arrivals from the prior best of 48 to 1+2/(1-alpha), which approaches 3 in the high-concurrency (small-alpha) regime that matches production serving. - The missing: All experiments assume perfectly known or well-classified output lengths (predictor accuracy/calibration under distribution shift is never measured), and the extensive theory (Poisson bounds, 1-bit distortion bounds) is validated only qualitatively via trend-matching plots, not with measured error against the derived closed-form expressions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or tuning LLM inference schedulers who wants a principled replacement for SJF with matching vLLM integration; the theory sections are dense but the practical takeaway (sort by 2D volume, not by predicted length) is actionable without following every proof.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) drive the score: the volume-certificate proof technique tightens the burst-arrival competitive ratio from 48 to a constant approaching 3, and the 1-bit SVF variant shows a single classification bit plus a minimax-derived proxy length guarantees an O(T)-bounded ratio. Rigor (62) is helped by public code release and vLLM integration but the Poisson and 1-bit distortion theorems are validated only via qualitative curve-shape agreement, not numeric bound-checking, and no seeds are reported. Completeness (62) and Resilience (62) are limited by the theory assuming known output lengths while the deployed system uses noisy predictors with no propagation analysis. Practical Impact (58) reflects a released, plug-and-play scheduler. Weighted: 0.25*72+0.20*62+0.25*72+0.10*62+0.15*62+0.05*58 = 67.
theoretical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive LLM serving under continuous batching is memory-constrained: each request's KV-cache footprint grows linearly with decode length while GPU memory is fixed, so an admission scheduler that packs requests well determines both average and tail latency. Prior work reduced this to a 1D scheduling problem and applied classical time-centric heuristics (FCFS, SJF), but a companion paper cited here (Wang, Ye & Zhou, [10]) proved SJF has an unbounded worst-case competitive ratio in this setting, exposing a gap between 1D scheduling theory and the actual 2D spatio-temporal geometry of LLM memory consumption. Key Idea: The core insight is that a request's scheduling priority should be its total memory-time volume (the area under its memory-occupancy curve over its lifetime), not its output length alone. Volume v_i = s_i·o_i + (o_i^2+o_i)/2 captures both the prompt's flat memory allocation and the decode phase's linearly growing footprint; sorting by this quantity, rather than by o_i (SJF) or arrival time (FCFS), aligns the scheduler's objective with the LP-relaxation-derived optimal packing rule the paper proves in Section 3.2. Methodology: The pipeline has two decoupled processes: (1) a background batched predictor (BERT-base regression for SVF, BERT-tiny binary classifier for 1-bit SVF) that estimates output length or a short/long class for queued requests, and (2) an admission loop that pops the lowest-volume request from a priority queue whenever KV-cache capacity allows, feeding vLLM's execution engine. For 1-bit SVF, class-conditional proxy lengths O_0, O_1 are theoretically derived (minimax geometric-mean design, threshold theta = sqrt(T), O_0 = T^{1/4}, O_1 = T^{3/4}) rather than fit empirically. Three design decisions stand out: (a) using an explicit greedy metric instead of a learned policy, justified in Appendix B as robustness against non-stationary/OOD traffic bursts; (b) the non-preemptive execution abstraction, which sidesteps KV-cache eviction/recomputation costs but also means the theory does not cover preemptive engines; (c) the volume-certificate proof technique (charging blocked waiting time to provable memory-occupancy mass) which is the paper's main theoretical contribution and yields a tighter bound than prior LP-relaxation or batch-partitioning arguments. Key Results: Under adversarial burst arrival, SVF's worst-case competitive ratio is 1+2/(1-alpha) where alpha is the peak per-request memory fraction, improving the prior constant of 48 (from [10]) to 3 as alpha to 0 (Table 1: alpha=1/100 gives CR<=3.02). Under Poisson arrivals (Theorem 3.4), SVF's expected competitive ratio is 1+2/((1-alpha)(1-mu)(1-rho)). Empirically (8B and 70B Llama-3.1, LMSYS-Chat and LongBench, 2,000 sampled requests each, 8x A100 80GB), SVF achieves the lowest average per-token latency across all four (model x dataset) combinations under burst arrival (e.g., 0.89 s/tok vs. FCFS's 2.46 and SJF's 1.01 on 8B/LMSYS), and 1-bit SVF achieves the highest throughput (3361.8 tok/s on 8B/LMSYS, vs. SVF's 3239.4 and SJF's 3229.0). Predictor overhead is under 0.06% of end-to-end latency (Table 3). No comparison against non-SJF/FCFS production schedulers (e.g., learning-to-rank methods cited in related work) is reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (SVF and 1-bit SVF as volume-based greedy admission policies) plus NEW FORMULATION (a tighter competitive-ratio proof technique for the KV-cache-constrained online scheduling model of [9]). The 2D volume metric itself is not conceptually new (it follows directly from the memory-time integral already formalized in cited prior work [9,10]); the genuine novelty is the volume-certificate proof achieving a constant-3 bound (down from 48) and the 1-bit proxy-length construction with a provable O(T) degradation cap. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is released at a GitHub URL (https://github.com/Aurora-Kl/Geometry-Aware-Online-Scheduling.git). The empirical section includes ablations against Oracle-SJF/Oracle-SVF (ground-truth-length upper bounds) and profiles predictor overhead, but reports no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed variance for any latency/throughput number in Table 2 or Figures 2-3, despite request sampling being randomized. All Poisson-arrival and 1-bit distortion theorems (3.4, 3.5) are validated only via qualitative curve-shape agreement in Figure 3, not by numerically checking the derived bound against measured competitive ratios. The predictor uses ground-truth output lengths for training data drawn from a held-out split of the same two benchmarks used for evaluation, which is standard practice but still means generalization to unseen workload distributions (the paper's own stated motivation for preferring greedy metrics over learned policies) is asserted, not tested. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (with a THEORETICAL component). Practical: Yes, evaluated on commercially available 8-GPU A100 nodes and integrated as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM. Efficient: Predictor overhead is under 0.06% of end-to-end time (Table 3); no comparison of SVF/1-bit SVF's own scheduling compute cost against SJF's is given, though both are O(n log n) sorts. Effective: Table 2 shows real per-token latency and throughput gains over FCFS and SJF baselines on two production-representative datasets, not just proxy metrics, though only two model sizes were tested. Scalable: Untested beyond 8 GPUs and 4,096-token max generation length; the paper explicitly flags multi-GPU cluster extension as future work, so 10x-100x scale (e.g., 100+ GPU clusters, longer contexts) is unverified. Strengths: - The volume-certificate proof (Lemma E.2, charging blocked-request waiting time against a lower-bound on OPT via prefix-volume sums) is a distinct proof architecture from prior LP-relaxation and time-window-discretization approaches, and produces a materially tighter constant (3 vs. 48). - The 1-bit variant demonstrates that a single binary classification bit, combined with a theoretically derived minimax proxy length, is sufficient to guarantee an O(T)-bounded (not unbounded) competitive ratio, a meaningfully cheaper alternative to full regression-based length prediction. - System-level validation (vLLM integration, overhead profiling under N=200 concurrent requests) grounds the theory in a deployable artifact rather than leaving it as pure analysis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state the current implementation is single-worker only and that extending to multi-GPU clusters requires new load-balancing strategies to handle inter-worker communication and evolving 2D memory footprints across nodes (Section 5, Conclusion). They also note in Appendix C that higher-throughput serving could lower the cost of generating misinformation or harmful content, recommending pairing with content moderation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The theoretical bounds all assume the true output length is known or falls within a stated interval (Section 3.1: "we operate under the standard theoretical assumption that the output generation length of each request is known"); the empirical section then uses learned predictors, but no result quantifies how prediction error propagates into the realized competitive ratio, leaving a gap between the clean theory and the noisy deployed system. - The 70B model's LMSYS results show 1-bit SVF beating SVF on tail latency and throughput (noted honestly in the text), but this reversal is not explained mechanistically. It is an unexamined interaction between predictor precision and workload composition. - No comparison against other prediction-based or learning-to-rank schedulers cited in the paper's own related-work section (e.g., [21], [26], [29]), only against FCFS and SJF, so the marginal value of the geometry-aware framing versus other post-SJF scheduling advances in the literature is not established. Bottom Line: Practitioners tuning LLM inference schedulers gain a theoretically grounded and empirically validated replacement for SJF (SVF and its 1-bit variant), but the paper does not establish that this specific volume-based approach outperforms other recent non-SJF prediction-based schedulers, so it should be read as a strong entry in a crowded space rather than a definitive new baseline.
Search Results 2606.22327 PDF
#18 — Innovative (67)

Multimodal Continuous Reasoning via Asymmetric Mutual Variational Learning (16 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Asymmetric Mutual Variational Learning (AMVL), a bidirectional KL-calibration training objective that lets a multimodal LLM discover continuous latent reasoning states without hand-crafted supervision while suppressing train-inference mismatch. - Why it's important: Discrete chain-of-thought forces high-dimensional visual reasoning through a lossy token vocabulary, and prior continuous-latent methods either need hand-crafted reconstruction targets or let the training-time posterior cheat by peeking at the answer, producing a prior that cannot reproduce those shortcuts at inference. - How it does it: A forward KL term pulls a target-agnostic prior toward the target-aware posterior's latent states (stop-gradient on the posterior), while a novel reverse KL term simultaneously regularizes the posterior against drifting into prior-incompatible, answer-leaking regions (stop-gradient on the prior). - The good: A formal proposition (Prop. 2, with proof in Appendix C.4) shows the equilibrium leakage coefficient shrinks by a factor of 1/(1+cγ/σ²_eff) under AMVL relative to one-sided ELBO training, giving a theoretical account of why the dual-KL design should reduce answer leakage, not just an empirical assertion. - The missing: All results are on a single backbone (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct) and a narrow benchmark family (V*, HRBench, BLINK, VisualPuzzles); there is no evaluation on open-ended generation, general VQA, or a second backbone/scale to show the effect survives model-size changes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on latent-space or continuous chain-of-thought reasoning in MLLMs, especially anyone using reference-answer-conditioned posteriors (e.g., RAVR-style setups) who wants a principled, theoretically motivated fix for posterior answer leakage rather than an ad hoc reconstruction loss.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile (Propositions 1-2 with proofs are central to the contribution). Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) drive the score: the asymmetric bidirectional-KL objective reframes latent-reasoning discovery as answer-leakage-aware variational calibration, and the closed-form leakage-shrinkage factor (1/(1+c gamma/sigma^2_eff)) plus the counter-intuitive forward-KL-only collapse (V* 40.84 below the 81.15 NTP baseline) are genuinely surprising results, matching the user's positive taste for principled mechanism papers. Rigor (60) is limited by proof-honesty caveats the authors do confront (the reverse-KL term is an EUBO surrogate), but no seeds/variance, a single 7B backbone, no code, and no direct leakage measurement (only downstream deltas). Resilience (62) and Completeness (62) are moderate: the leakage claim is never measured directly. Practical Impact (55) for a training-objective formulation with no released artifact, though the theory points toward a concrete algorithm. Weighted (theoretical): 0.25*72 + 0.20*60 + 0.25*75 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*62 + 0.05*55 = 67.
theoretical Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: MLLMs that reason via discrete Chain-of-Thought must verbalize continuous, high-dimensional visual concepts into a fixed vocabulary, a "language-space bottleneck" that causes reasoning drift and hallucination on fine-grained spatial or multi-step visual tasks (e.g., dense high-resolution perception, visual jigsaw/spatial-relation puzzles). Recent continuous-latent-reasoning methods (LVR, Monet, Mull-Tokens) avoid this bottleneck but require explicit hand-crafted supervision (reconstruction or alignment losses) to shape the latent states, constraining what the latent can encode. A more principled formulation treats latent reasoning as variational inference with a target-agnostic prior (used at inference) and a target-aware posterior (used at training, conditioned on the ground-truth answer). This introduces train-inference mismatch: the posterior can exploit answer-dependent shortcuts unavailable at test time, and standard forward-KL (ELBO) training pulls the prior toward this contaminated posterior, degrading inference-time latent quality. Prior work in this space (e.g., RAVR/ReGuLaR) does not correct this leakage. Key Idea: Reformulate the standard ELBO's one-sided forward-KL prior-to-posterior training as a two-sided, differently-conditioned calibration: a forward KL that teaches the prior to track the posterior, plus a reverse KL that constrains the posterior itself away from prior-incompatible (answer-leaking) latent regions. Unlike symmetric deep mutual learning, the two KL terms operate on fundamentally asymmetric distributions (target-agnostic vs. target-aware, inference vs. training role), and each loss has a stop-gradient on the other side so the two updates target different parameter sets. Methodology: The model inserts k=8 latent placeholder tokens between the multimodal prompt x and target y in the input sequence. Final hidden states at these positions parameterize diagonal Gaussian prior p_θ(Z|x) and posterior q_φ(Z|x,y) via a lightweight shared variational head (SwiGLU + RMSNorm projection to mean and log-variance, latent dim d=512). Latents are sampled via the standard reparameterization trick and injected back into the decoder through a linear/MLP "latent injector," conditioning autoregressive answer generation. The composite loss is L_total = L_NTP + βL_fwd + γL_rev, where L_NTP is next-token prediction conditioned on a posterior latent sample, L_fwd = D_KL(sg[q_φ]‖p_θ) trains only the prior (stop-gradient on posterior), and L_rev = D_KL(sg[p_θ]‖q_φ) trains only the posterior against the (frozen) learned prior as a reference — the latter is explicitly framed as an EUBO-motivated practical surrogate rather than the intractable true reverse KL against p_θ(Z|x,y). Both KL terms have closed forms for diagonal Gaussians. β and γ are scheduled asymmetrically: β warms up early so the prior tracks the posterior, γ is delayed so the posterior isn't over-regularized before the prior is calibrated. Three design decisions stand out: (1) using the learned prior (not the intractable true posterior) as the reverse-KL reference is a pragmatic approximation whose quality depends entirely on how well the prior is calibrated at that point in training — a potential circularity the paper's Appendix B.2 acknowledges but does not fully resolve; (2) stop-gradients enforce a clean asymmetric division of labor, well-justified by Proposition 1's closed-form gradient analysis; (3) k=8 latent tokens and d=512 are chosen via ablation (Table 4) rather than derived analytically, with degradation reported at both larger k (16) and larger d (768), attributed informally to "information dilution" and "over-parameterization" without a mechanistic account. Key Results: Built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, AMVL reaches a 74.97 average Overall score across V, HRBench4K, and HRBench8K (Table 1), a +5.57 absolute gain over the Qwen2.5-VL-7B base and best among all open-source baselines including SFT/GRPO variants, discrete-CoT methods (Vision-R1, PAPO, PixelReasoner, DeepEyes) and other continuous-latent methods (LVR, Mull-Tokens, Monet). On BLINK (Table 2) it averages 66.91 (+10.83 over base), with a +32.00 point jump on Jigsaw (26.00 to a claimed level implied by the delta) and a 32.67 IQ Test score, both areas where discrete verbalization underperforms. Ablations (Table 3) show NTP-only training is weak (81.15 V, 70.50/67.38 HRBench), forward-KL-only training is markedly worse than the base NTP objective alone (40.84 V), reverse-KL-only partially helps (75.92 V), and the full NTP+Fwd-KL+Rev-KL combination (forward-first scheduling) achieves the best results (84.29/72.12/68.50), with a same-magnitude ablation showing scheduling order (forward-first vs. reverse-first) itself changes V* by 3.66 points (84.29 vs. 80.63). Baselines are current 2025-2026 MLLM reasoning methods on the same Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone, which is a fair, matched comparison; GPT-4o is included only as a reference point and is not the primary comparison target. All evaluation is on public benchmarks at standard scale (7B parameter model, established test sets); no training or inference cost/throughput numbers are reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the asymmetric bidirectional KL objective) and NEW FORMULATION (framing latent reasoning discovery as answer-leakage-aware variational calibration, with an accompanying theoretical decomposition of leakage into a residual bias term). The variational-head architecture, reparameterization sampling, and MLLM latent-injection scheme are standard components combined in a new way; the genuinely novel piece is the reverse-KL regularizer and its EUBO motivation plus the accompanying leakage-contamination proofs (Propositions 1–2), which the paper explicitly contrasts against one-sided ELBO training used by prior continuous-latent-reasoning work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and directly interrogate the claimed mechanism: they isolate forward-only, reverse-only, and full objectives (Table 3), and separately sweep latent token count and dimension (Table 4), which is the right ablation structure for this kind of claim. However, no error bars, multiple random seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere in the results tables, so gains of 1-2 points (e.g., HRBench8K FSP 79.75 vs. 78.00 for Mull-Tokens) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run noise. No code, data, or model-weight release URL is mentioned in the visible text. Training data (Visual-CoT, ReFocus, CogCoM, Zebra-CoT) is a mixture of established datasets rather than a self-constructed benchmark, which mitigates one form of Goodhart's-Law risk, but evaluation is confined to benchmarks the authors selected, and no held-out or adversarial-latent-manipulation test is used to directly verify the answer-leakage claim empirically (the paper's evidence for reduced leakage is a downstream benchmark score, not a direct measurement of latent-mutual-information or reconstruction accuracy under posterior-vs-prior swap at inference). Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper's primary claims rest on benchmark results from a trained, evaluated system). - Practical: Yes for research settings — built on a 7B open-source backbone with lightweight added variational heads (linear/SwiGLU projections), no unusual hardware requirement stated. - Efficient: Not stated precisely; the added components are described as "lightweight," but no FLOPs, latency, or memory overhead numbers versus the base Qwen2.5-VL-7B or versus LVR/Monet/Mull-Tokens are reported. - Effective: The evidence (Tables 1-2) shows improved benchmark accuracy over both discrete and other continuous-latent baselines on the tested tasks, which is a reasonably direct measure of the stated problem (fine-grained perception and spatial reasoning), though it is still a proxy for the underlying claim about reduced answer leakage rather than a direct measurement of that leakage. - Scalable: Not tested. All experiments use a single 7B backbone and k=8/d=512 latent configuration; there is no evaluation at larger model scale or with substantially larger latent budgets beyond the reported degradation at k=16/d=768, so claims about the approach scaling to larger reasoning budgets or larger backbones are unsupported by direct evidence. The paper delivers a training-objective formulation integrated into an existing MLLM architecture; no standalone software artifact, dataset, or released model checkpoint is mentioned in the available sections. Strengths: - The Propositions 1-2 provide a closed-form theoretical account (for diagonal Gaussian latents) of how reverse-KL regularization suppresses posterior drift and reduces prior mean contamination by an explicit multiplicative factor, going beyond a purely empirical justification for the reverse-KL addition. - The ablation in Table 3 isolating forward-KL-only training reveals a genuinely informative and non-obvious failure mode (V* collapses to 40.84, well below the NTP-only baseline of 81.15), providing direct empirical support for the paper's claim that one-sided calibration is actively harmful, not merely insufficient. - The asymmetric KL scheduling (forward warm-up before reverse introduction) is shown to matter concretely: the same set of loss terms with reversed scheduling order costs 3.66 V* points, which is a specific, testable design contribution rather than a generic tuning detail. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The visible sections (through page 17 of the theoretical appendix) do not include an explicit "Limitations" section; the paper does note in Appendix B.2 that the reverse-KL regularizer is a "practical surrogate" for the intractable true EUBO reverse divergence against p_θ(Z|x,y), and confronts this gap directly rather than presenting L_rev as an exact theoretical optimum. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No direct measurement of answer leakage itself (e.g., mutual information between posterior latents and the answer, or accuracy when swapping in the prior at training time) is reported; the leakage-reduction claim rests entirely on downstream benchmark deltas and the closed-form Gaussian analysis, not on an empirical leakage metric. - Single backbone (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and no larger-scale replication; given how sensitive latent-token count and dimension are shown to be (Table 4), it is unclear whether k=8/d=512 remains near-optimal for a substantially larger or smaller backbone. - No statistical uncertainty (seeds, variance) is reported despite several close-margin comparisons against LVR/PixelReasoner/Monet in Tables 1-2, so some claimed "best-in-class" sub-metric wins may not be robust. - No inference-time latency/throughput comparison against discrete-CoT baselines, despite the paper's framing implying continuous latent reasoning should be more compute-efficient than verbose text CoT. Bottom Line: A researcher building latent-space or continuous chain-of-thought reasoning systems for MLLMs gains a theoretically grounded, empirically validated fix for posterior answer leakage that measurably outperforms both discrete-CoT and prior hand-crafted-supervision latent baselines on a matched 7B backbone, though the single-backbone, single-benchmark-family scope and absence of direct leakage measurement or variance reporting mean the generality of the gains beyond this exact setup remains unverified.
Recommended 2607.00461 PDF
#19 — Innovative (67)

NormAct: A Benchmark for Hidden Social Norm Compliance in Embodied Planning

S Zhao, X Song, T Guo, X Gao, M Liu, X Han, Y Zhang… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NormAct, a 550-episode embodied-planning benchmark that hides an unstated social norm inside an ordinary task goal and separately scores Goal Achieved, Norm Compliance, and Task Success on the generated action sequence, plus NormPerceptor, a fine-tuned cue generator that infers scene-grounded social context before planning. - Why it's important: Deploying MLLM-based embodied planners (service robots, home assistants) that complete tasks while violating unstated social constraints (privacy, ownership, queueing, resource stewardship) risks real-world social harm even when the literal goal is achieved. - How it does it: Every scene encodes both the physical affordances needed for the goal and separate visual evidence for a hidden norm, so the two are scored with independent binary rewards on the action trajectory rather than on the model's free-form explanation, and NormPerceptor is SFT-trained (Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct) on GPT-4o-labeled first-person images to emit a short norm-aware cue before the planner acts. - The good: In the no-cue setting, the three evaluated MLLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) achieve the explicit goal in 67.3% of 1,650 trials but comply with the hidden norm in only 26.4%, with the largest single outcome class (751/1,650 trials) being goal achieved and norm violated, a clean and reproducible demonstration that goal completion metrics overstate social competence. - The missing: The benchmark evaluates only short (at most three-turn) action sequences over 11 task types built on one simulator (TongSim) with synthetic scenes, so it is unclear whether the norm-inference gap persists over long-horizon tasks, in physical (non-simulated) deployments, or in cultural contexts outside those the authors curated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating embodied MLLM agents for human-populated environments, since it isolates a failure mode (norm activation and grounding, not norm knowledge) that goal-completion benchmarks like BEHAVIOR-1K or EmbodiedBench do not measure; less relevant to text-only norm-judgment researchers already covered by Social Chemistry 101 or Moral Stories.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (78) and Non-Obviousness (72) are the top contributors: the goal/norm reward decomposition and graded-cue diagnostic isolate norm grounding (not norm knowledge) as the bottleneck, a non-obvious result, and the limitations are honest and non-minimizing. Novelty (72) reflects a new benchmark plus a genuinely new evaluation formulation, though all components (MLLM planners, SFT, RAG, TongSim) are standard. Rigor (58) is the main drag: no seeds, CIs, or significance tests anywhere, n=50 per task-cue cell, a thin 50-case LLM-audit, and proprietary frontier planners with uncontrolled training data, partly offset by careful leakage control and a public dataset. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*62 = 67.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 78 Resilience 65 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Embodied MLLM planners operating in first-person, human-populated environments (homes, shared spaces) are typically evaluated only on whether they achieve an explicitly stated goal. Real deployments require the agent to additionally respect implicit social norms never stated in the instruction, such as waiting in line, asking permission before using someone's belongings, or turning off a faucet after use. Prior text-based norm benchmarks (Social Chemistry 101, Moral Stories) test norm judgment on written scenarios without an embodied action component, and prior multimodal norm benchmarks (NormLens, EgoNormia) test whether a model can judge or explain whether a depicted behavior is acceptable, not whether an agent autonomously infers and enforces a norm while executing a task. Embodied benchmarks (BEHAVIOR-1K, EmbodiedBench) measure task completion and efficiency without scoring social appropriateness. No prior benchmark evaluates whether hidden social norms are realized in an agent's own action sequence during ordinary task execution. Key Idea: Decouple goal achievement from norm compliance as two independent binary rewards computed over the same generated action trajectory, so a plan can be scored as competent-but-antisocial (goal achieved, norm violated) rather than collapsing both into a single success signal. The paper further uses a graded cue protocol (no cue to specific cue) as a diagnostic instrument to localize whether failures trace to missing norm knowledge, failure to activate/ground the norm in the visual scene, or failure to translate an activated norm into the correct executable action. Methodology: The pipeline has three components. (1) Automated scene generation on the TongSim 3D platform constructs, for each of 11 task templates (5 norm dimensions: public rules, etiquette/interaction, resource responsibility, privacy/ownership, social relationship), a first-person RGB+segmentation observation containing both the affordances for an explicit goal and the visual evidence for a co-located hidden norm; 50 instances per task type yield 550 episodes. (2) Evaluation protocol: a planner receives (observation, goal, prompt condition, action-space API) and outputs a structured JSON action sequence; R_goal and R_norm are computed as indicator functions over the trajectory (Eq. 1-3), and Task Success requires both. Six prompt conditions of increasing informativeness are compared: no cue, category cue (abstract norm domain), specific cue (human-written scene-specific rule), evidence cue (perceptual evidence made salient, rule not stated), RAG cue (retrieval from NormBank, a 150k-entry situational-norm knowledge base, via a FAISS-indexed bge-base-v1.5 embedding + bge-reranker-base two-stage retrieval), and generated cue (NormPerceptor's automatic inference). (3) NormPerceptor is Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct fine-tuned via SFT (60 epochs, batch size 4, lr 1e-4) on 1,100 image-goal-to-cue pairs where the images are Seedance 2.0-generated (independent of benchmark test scenes) and labels are GPT-4o-generated norm descriptions, so N_i = P_theta(o_i^rgb, g_i) is fed to the fixed base planner (Gemini 3 Pro) alongside the observation and goal. Critical design decisions: (a) using per-instance binary rewards over the full trajectory rather than final-state checking, so intermediate norm violations (e.g., stepping outside a crosswalk mid-route) are penalized, which is well justified for process-oriented social evaluation; (b) fixing Gemini 3 Pro as the base planner for the evidence/RAG/generated-cue experiments because it is the strongest base model, which is reasonable for isolating cue effects but leaves cue transfer to weaker planners untested (acknowledged limitation); (c) an automated GPT-4o failure-cause classifier over four error types (norm inference, cue-to-action, perception-grounding, goal-norm tradeoff), validated by manual review of 50 sampled cases per cue condition with no identified errors, though 50 samples is a modest audit size for a 550-1650-trial dataset. Key Results: Aggregate over 3 models x 550 trials each (1,650 trials) in the no-cue condition: Goal Achieved 67.3%, Norm Compliance 26.4%, Task Success 21.8%. Category cues raise these to 78.4%/43.5%/33.9%; specific cues to 71.6%/63.9%/49.6% (Figure 2, Table 3 reports the Gemini-only breakdown: no-cue 77.3/26.7/24.2, category 78.4/49.3/41.6, specific 71.6/70.7/56.5). With Gemini 3 Pro fixed as base planner: evidence cues reach 67.5% Goal Achieved / 67.1% Norm Compliance / 50.2% Task Success; RAG cues reach the highest Goal Achieved (83.6%) but the lowest Norm Compliance among cue conditions (24.5%) and Task Success (23.1%), i.e., RAG cues do not improve over the no-cue baseline; generated cues (NormPerceptor) reach 76.2%/50.0%/46.7%. Task-level results (Table 4) show extreme cases: returning shared objects and washing used dishes have 0.0% no-cue Norm Compliance despite 62.7% and 64.7% no-cue Goal Achieved respectively; avoiding private rooms reaches 94.0% Norm Compliance under evidence cue but only 20.0% Task Success. Error analysis (Figure 3) shows norm-inference failures drop from 757 to 204 under specific cue, while goal-norm tradeoff failures rise from 111 to 380, and cue-to-action failures stay comparatively stable (160 to 126). Comparisons are against the paper's own no-cue/category-cue/RAG baselines; there is no comparison to non-LLM planners or to prior embodied benchmarks' baseline agents, since NormAct is a new task with no established prior methods. Scale is standard (three current commercial-grade MLLMs, 550-1,650 trials), not stress-scale. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (NormAct, pairing ordinary embodied goals with hidden, scene-grounded social norms and separate goal/norm reward decomposition) and NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (NormPerceptor, a lightweight SFT cue generator for automatic norm activation). The evaluation protocol (binary rewards over action trajectories, graded cue diagnostic) is a genuinely new formulation for this problem; the underlying components (MLLM planners, SFT fine-tuning of a VLM, RAG with FAISS/bge-reranker, TongSim/Seedance for scene generation) are standard techniques applied to a new setting rather than novel methods in themselves. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation-style analysis is present in the form of the six-condition cue comparison and the four-category error decomposition, which functions as a form of component attribution. No error bars, confidence intervals, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any of the percentage metrics (a notable omission given the finer task-level breakdowns in Table 4 have only 50 trials per task-cue cell, so single-run percentages carry substantial sampling noise, e.g., differences like 32.0% vs 35.3% queue-waiting Norm Compliance could plausibly reflect noise rather than a stable cue effect). The failure-cause classifier itself is GPT-4o (not a disinterested third-party judge, and not the same models being evaluated, which avoids strict self-evaluation but is still an LLM auditing other LLMs), validated on only 50 manually reviewed cases per cue condition (out of up to 1,650), a relatively thin audit. Benchmark data (via HuggingFace) is stated to be publicly available; code/weights for NormPerceptor are not confirmed as released beyond the dataset link. No Goodhart's Law risk in the strict sense since evaluation and generation scenes are separated for NormPerceptor (explicitly designed to avoid leakage), but the main benchmark's evaluated models (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) are proprietary frontier models whose training data overlap with NormBank or TongSim-adjacent content cannot be ruled out. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark evaluation of existing MLLMs plus an SFT-trained auxiliary cue model, no formal proofs). Practical: Yes, evaluated models are commercial API-accessible MLLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) and NormPerceptor is a 2B-parameter model, all runnable on standard cloud/consumer inference hardware. Efficient: NormPerceptor adds one extra lightweight VLM inference call per episode before planning; RAG cues add a two-stage FAISS retrieval plus reranking step over a 150k-entry corpus, which is more expensive but still standard retrieval-inference cost; the paper does not report latency or token-cost numbers for any condition. Effective: The evidence shows models can be prompted to comply with norms when the norm is stated or its evidence highlighted (up to 70.7% Norm Compliance with specific cue), but the primary practical target, unprompted (no-cue) compliance, remains at 26.4% aggregate, indicating the core problem (autonomous hidden-norm compliance) is not solved, only diagnosed. Scalable: Untested. The benchmark covers 11 task types and short (at most three-turn) action sequences; the paper explicitly states this does not cover long-horizon social interaction, and no experiments probe larger scenes, longer horizons, or more task categories. The work delivers two software artifacts: the NormAct benchmark dataset (publicly released on HuggingFace) and the NormPerceptor cue-generation model (methodology and training data described, code/weight release not explicitly confirmed in the visible text). Strengths: - The goal/norm reward decomposition (Eq. 1-3) is a clean, generalizable evaluation primitive that cleanly separates two conflated capabilities (task competence, social appropriateness) that prior embodied benchmarks bundle into a single completion metric. - The graded cue protocol (no cue -> category -> specific -> evidence -> RAG -> generated) functions as a genuine diagnostic instrument, not just an ablation, and produces an interpretable causal chain: RAG cues (generic norm knowledge) fail to help while evidence cues (scene grounding of a known norm) help substantially, isolating norm grounding, not norm knowledge, as the bottleneck. - Process-oriented reward computation (norm compliance is checked throughout the trajectory, e.g., stepping off a crosswalk mid-route in the road-crossing task, not just at the final state) is a more rigorous design than typical final-state-only embodied benchmarks. - Careful leakage control between NormPerceptor's training data (Seedance-generated images, independent scene source) and the benchmark's evaluation scenes (TongSim), directly addressing a common contamination risk in benchmark-adjacent auxiliary models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state the benchmark currently has only 11 task types and uses short, high-level action sequences, explicitly not covering the full range of long-horizon social interaction. They note the evidence/RAG/generated-cue experiments use only Gemini 3 Pro as the fixed base planner, so it is unknown whether generated or retrieved cues transfer to weaker or differently calibrated planners. They acknowledge the generated-cue results (NormPerceptor) leave room for improvement, remaining below human-written specific cues, especially on privacy, queueing, and resource-use tasks. They also flag cultural/demographic bias risk (norms vary across cultures and NormAct may underrepresent some) and recommend the benchmark be used as a diagnostic tool rather than a deployment-readiness certificate. These are direct, non-minimizing acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No confidence intervals, seeds, or statistical tests are reported anywhere, so many of the fine-grained task-level and cue-condition differences (particularly the non-monotonic patterns the authors themselves flag, e.g., queue-waiting Norm Compliance rising then falling under specific cue) cannot be distinguished from single-run noise at n=50 per cell. The failure-cause taxonomy and its manual audit (50 of up to 1,650 cases) is thin relative to the scale of claims drawn from it. All three evaluated planners are proprietary frontier models (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) with unknown, uncontrolled training data; the paper cannot rule out that some norm-relevant knowledge or even TongSim/NormBank-adjacent content leaked into pretraining, which would bias no-cue baselines in an unknown direction. The RAG baseline uses a single, fixed retrieval configuration (NormBank, top-k=5, one embedding/reranker pair); no sweep over retrieval depth, corpus choice, or prompt formatting is reported, so the negative RAG result, while interesting, is not shown to generalize across RAG configurations. The paper does not report human-baseline compliance rates on the same tasks, so there is no anchor for how far 26.4% no-cue Norm Compliance is from a plausible human-level ceiling. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating embodied MLLM planners for human environments gains a concrete, reusable evaluation methodology and a reproducible empirical finding, frontier MLLMs achieve stated goals 67.3% of the time but comply with co-located hidden social norms only 26.4% of the time, that is not measured by any prior embodied or norm-judgment benchmark; the underlying idea generalizes beyond this specific benchmark to any embodied-agent evaluation that currently conflates task completion with situational appropriateness.
Search Results 2606.27826 PDF
#20 — Innovative (67)

Agentic Abstention: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act? (120 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Agentic Abstention, a formal POMDP framing of when a tool-using agent should stop acting rather than continue, builds a 28,000-instance benchmark spanning web shopping, terminal execution, and interactive QA, evaluates 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 scaffolds on it, and proposes CONVOLVE, a context-engineering method that distills interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules. - Why it's important: Agents that keep acting on infeasible or underspecified tasks waste tool calls, consume external resources (search quota, terminal state, purchases), and produce misleading completions, so knowing when to stop is as important as knowing how to act. - How it does it: The paper redefines abstention as a sequential, multi-turn decision (rather than a single-turn answer-or-abstain call) using an oracle 'warranted step' w_i per episode, then measures timely versus delayed versus failed abstention via AbsRec@K, and CONVOLVE has a reflection model review trajectories and a curator model turn those reflections into a token-budgeted playbook appended to the agent's system prompt. - The good: CONVOLVE raises Llama-3.3-70B's timely abstention recall from 26.7% to 57.4% and overall recall from 83.2% to 100.0% on WebShop using only 20 training trajectories, and lessons distilled from an 8B model transfer to improve the 70B model nearly as much as the 70B model's own self-generated lessons (57.4/100.0/78.9 SPL vs. 55.3/99.0/76.4 SPL). - The missing: CONVOLVE is evaluated only in the WebShop setting in the main text (Terminal-Bench and AbstentionBench results are relegated to an appendix not read in full here), and the benchmark's environment-based abstention tasks cover only two failure modes (missing target, missing prerequisite), leaving stale resources, permission boundaries, and conflicting tool outputs unaddressed by construction. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating tool-using LLM agents, especially those designing safety/reliability evaluations, because it reframes abstention as a POMDP timing problem with a concrete, reusable metric (AbsRec@K) rather than treating it as a single-turn classification task.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (76) leads: it formalizes agentic multi-turn abstention as a POMDP with an oracle warranted-step and a timing-aware metric (AbsRec@K), which is a genuine new problem plus formulation, though CONVOLVE is largely an application of ACE/Reflexion-style context evolution. Non-Obviousness (72) and Completeness (72) are supported by the environment-based abstention construction (modifying the environment, not the instruction) and honest, itemized scope limitations including contamination risk. Rigor (56) is pulled down by single-run headline numbers with no seeds/CIs and no ablation isolating the reflection versus curator contributions; Resilience (62) is limited because CONVOLVE's main-text evidence is scoped to WebShop and the premature-abstention penalty is a consequential, undiscussed scoring choice. Weighted: 0.20*76 + 0.25*56 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*64 = 67.
empirical Novelty 76 Rigor 56 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 64
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents that browse the web, operate terminals, or answer questions via search are expected to act autonomously over many turns, but not every instruction is well-specified or achievable given the agent's tools and environment. Standard LLM abstention research treats the decision as a single-turn answer-or-refuse classification given a static prompt. In agentic settings this framing breaks down: the agent has a much richer action space (search, click, act, or stop), and whether a task is infeasible may only become apparent after several turns of interaction with the environment (e.g., discovering a shopping catalog has no matching product, or a terminal environment is missing a required file). No prior benchmark evaluates whether agents recognize infeasibility as it unfolds during multi-turn interaction, nor whether they stop promptly once it does. Key Idea: Reframe abstention as a sequential decision problem in a POMDP where the ground-truth feasibility of a task is latent and each abstain-warranted episode has an oracle 'earliest warranted step' w_i; abstention quality is then measured not just by whether the agent eventually abstains, but by how close its abstention step is to w_i (captured by AbsRec@K and SPL). This decouples 'can the agent detect infeasibility at all' from 'does it stop as soon as evidence permits,' which the paper shows are empirically distinct capabilities. Methodology: The benchmark construction pipeline adapts three existing task sources: (1) WebShop, where 500 solvable instructions are retained and 500 new abstention-warranted instructions are constructed, split into Request-based Abstention (Subjective Preference, Underspecified Intent, False Premise/Contradiction — generated via LLM rewrite of instructions, manually filtered) and Environment-based Abstention (Missing Target — removing the ground-truth item from the product catalog and rebuilding the Lucene retrieval index, so infeasibility is discoverable only through interaction); (2) Terminal-Bench 2.0, adapted similarly via a dual-agent rewriting/validation pipeline for Request-based tasks (False Premise/Contradiction, Underspecified Intent) and manual environment modification for Missing Prerequisite tasks; (3) AbstentionBench, a 16-dataset, 27,073-sample QA aggregation converted into a sequential decision problem where the agent can answer, abstain, or issue a search call against an enwiki-20260101 dump (FlashRAG-chunked, E5-base dense retrieval, top-3 documents per call, max 10 calls). Evaluation metrics are formalized episode-level: AbsRec@K (fraction of abstain-warranted episodes where the agent abstains within K steps after warrant), Timely Recall (AbsRec@1), Overall Recall (AbsRec@10, since K_max=10), and SPL (success weighted by inverse path length, penalizing delayed abstention). CONVOLVE, the improvement method, runs one offline training epoch over WebShop abstention examples: a rollout produces a trajectory, a reflection model identifies abstention-relevant signals (did the agent keep acting after infeasibility, what revealed infeasibility, what delayed abstention), and a curator model (512-token generation limit, 6,000-token input truncation, 80,000-token total playbook budget) converts this into structured playbook updates appended to the agent's system prompt, with no model parameter updates. Three design decisions stand out: (1) using an LLM-based reflection+curation loop rather than fine-tuning is well-justified given the paper's data efficiency claim (20 trajectories suffice), (2) counting premature abstention (before w_i) as a failure is a defensible but consequential scoring choice that penalizes conservative agents equally with agents that never abstain, and (3) restricting environment-based infeasibility to only two categories (missing target, missing prerequisite) is a narrower operationalization of 'infeasibility discovered through interaction' than the framing implies. Key Results: Across 13 models/2 scaffolds on 28,000+ tasks, abstention recall is generally low and timely abstention is markedly worse than overall recall. In the web setting, 6 of 8 models tested achieve less than 0.5 AbsRec@10; the strongest baseline reaches only 26.7% timely recall (AbsRec@1) despite 83.2% overall recall (AbsRec@10) — a 56.5 percentage-point gap indicating agents mostly abstain only after unnecessary interaction. Llama-3.3-70B is the best web performer (~0.84 AbsRec@10). In the terminal setting, scaffold matters more than base model: with the same GPT-5.4-mini backbone, Codex CLI reaches ~0.38 AbsRec@10 versus Terminus 2's ~0.18, and the best terminal configuration reaches only 21.6% timely recall. In QA, Qwen3-235B performs best (0.59 AbsRec@1, ~0.71 AbsRec@10); several categories (False Premise, Underspecified Intent) stay at or below 50% recall for all models. Reasoning and scale show mixed effects: Qwen-3-235B-Thinking improves timely recall over Instruct but lowers overall recall (Figure 5), and scaling Qwen from 8B to 235B raises overall recall but leaves timely recall roughly flat (Figure 7), while over-abstention on solvable tasks climbs to 34% (Qwen3-235B-Instruct) or 24% (Thinking) by turn 10 in the web setting. For CONVOLVE, with only 20 training trajectories, Llama-3.3-8B's timely recall rises from 6.9% to 12.9% (self-lessons) and Llama-3.3-70B's rises from 26.7% to 57.4%, overall recall from 83.2% to 100.0%, and SPL from 55.3 to 78.9; critically, lessons learned by the 8B model transferred to the 70B model achieve nearly identical gains (55.3% timely, 99.0% overall, 76.4 SPL) to the 70B model's self-generated lessons, evidence that the distilled lessons are not just capturing what the larger model already implicitly knows. All numbers are self-reported by the authors on their own constructed benchmark; no comparison is made to an external, previously published abstention benchmark's headline numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM (formalizing agentic, multi-turn abstention as distinct from single-turn LLM abstention), NEW FORMULATION (POMDP framing with oracle warranted-step annotation), NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (28,000+ instance consolidated benchmark adapting WebShop, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and AbstentionBench), NEW METHOD (CONVOLVE, applying Agentic Context Engineering-style playbook distillation specifically to abstention). The genuinely novel piece is the timing-aware metric (AbsRec@K with oracle warranted step w_i) and the environment-based abstention construction (modifying environments rather than instructions); CONVOLVE itself is largely an application of existing context-evolution techniques (Reflexion, ACE) to a new target behavior rather than a new context-engineering mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility: The benchmark construction includes real validation: rewritten instructions are manually reviewed (up to three correction rounds for Terminal-Bench), and Figure 8/9 report length-distribution and t-SNE embedding similarity checks (similarity 0.899-0.972) to show rewritten instructions are not trivially distinguishable from originals, addressing a natural concern about artificial construction. However, CONVOLVE's headline numbers (Table 1) are reported for a single held-out set of 101 examples with no error bars, seeds, or significance tests, and no ablation isolates the separate contributions of the reflection model versus the curator model versus playbook size (80,000-token budget, 512-token curator limit) to the final gains. Code and data are stated to be released (https://lhannnn.github.io/agentic-abstention), which is a positive for reproducibility. The paper's own Appendix A flags a real limitation: possible benchmark contamination since components (WebShop, Terminal-Bench, AbstentionBench) are public and could appear in pretraining or post-training data, though it argues this is mitigated by constructing new abstention-warranted variants. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile (agent evaluations over public LLMs and scaffolds). - Practical: Yes, all evaluated agents run via standard first-party APIs (OpenAI, xAI) or Together AI/OpenRouter for open-weight models; CONVOLVE requires no fine-tuning infrastructure, only inference-time context injection. - Efficient: CONVOLVE is notably cheap relative to fine-tuning, needing only 20 training trajectories and one offline epoch, though the reflection+curation pipeline adds inference cost (extra LLM calls per rollout) not directly quantified in dollar or token terms in the main text. - Effective: The evidence shows the method solves the stated problem on WebShop specifically (timely recall +30.7 points, overall recall +16.8 points for the 70B model); whether this generalizes to the terminal or QA settings is only addressed in an appendix not reviewed here, so within the main text the effectiveness claim is scoped to one of three evaluated environments. - Scalable: Not tested; the paper does not report CONVOLVE at larger training-trajectory counts, larger models beyond 70B, or in settings with more diverse abstention categories, so the 10x-100x scaling behavior is unclear. The work delivers a benchmark and code release (per the project URL) rather than a trained model artifact; CONVOLVE is a prompting/context method usable directly with any agent that accepts a system-prompt playbook. Strengths: - The oracle warranted-step (w_i) annotation and the resulting AbsRec@K / timely-vs-overall recall distinction is a genuine methodological contribution that exposes a failure mode (agents eventually abstain but only after wasted turns) invisible to single-turn abstention metrics. - The environment-based abstention construction (modifying the environment rather than the instruction) is a non-obvious and directly relevant test of whether agents update beliefs from observations rather than pattern-matching on instruction phrasing. - The cross-scenario, cross-model breadth (13 systems, 2 scaffolds, 3 environments) supports the paper's central claim that abstention is shaped by scaffold and interaction structure, not just base model capability, which is a testable and falsifiable claim rather than a vague assertion. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state (Appendix A) that: real-world agents operate over richer interfaces, private tools, and longer-lived state than the three benchmarked settings; the environment-based abstention tasks cover only missing-target and missing-prerequisite failure modes, omitting permission boundaries, stale external resources, and conflicting tool outputs; the paper does not test every model/scaffold/reasoning-setting combination; and benchmark contamination from public source datasets cannot be fully ruled out. This is a direct and non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The main text does not report CONVOLVE ablations isolating the reflection step from the curation step, nor does it report CONVOLVE's effect outside WebShop within the reviewed pages (Terminal-Bench/AbstentionBench CONVOLVE results are deferred to an appendix). The paper does not report inference-cost or latency overhead of the reflection+curator pipeline relative to the abstention gains achieved, which matters for practical adoption. The premature-abstention penalty (treating any abstention before w_i as a failure) could systematically undercount agents that are conservative for good reasons not captured by the oracle's w_i definition, and the paper does not discuss sensitivity of its conclusions to this scoring convention. No comparison is made against non-context-engineering baselines for improving abstention (e.g., simple confidence thresholding, explicit chain-of-thought infeasibility checks), so it's unclear how much of CONVOLVE's gain is attributable to context evolution specifically versus any structured hint injection. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating tool-using agents gains a concrete, reusable evaluation methodology (AbsRec@K, oracle warranted steps) that exposes a real and under-measured failure mode, timely versus eventual abstention, though the CONVOLVE method's evidence is currently strong only for the WebShop setting and lacks ablations that would clarify which piece of the pipeline drives the reported 30-point recall gains.
Recommended 2606.28733 PDF
#21 — Innovative (66)

Multi-Resolution Flow Matching: Training-Free Diffusion Acceleration via Staged Sampling (21 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MrFlow, a training-free staged-sampling pipeline that accelerates pretrained flow-matching text-to-image models by generating structure at low resolution, super-resolving in pixel space, injecting low-strength noise, then refining at high resolution in a single step. - Why it's important: Flow-matching models like Qwen-Image-20B take up to 47 seconds per 1024x1024 image on an A100, and existing training-free accelerators (feature caching, token pruning, prior multi-resolution methods) either cap out below 4x speedup or introduce visible blurring/artifacts when pushed past 5x-8x. - How it does it: Low-strength noise injection (sigma in [0.1, 0.15]) after pixel-space GAN super-resolution acts as a frequency-selective filter, preserving the low-frequency structure already locked in at low resolution while letting a single high-resolution denoising step resample and correct only the high-frequency SR artifacts. - The good: On Qwen-Image, MrFlow holds GenEval at 0.86 (native 0.88) and OneIG-En/Zh within 1-2 points at 10.3x end-to-end speedup, and composes orthogonally with Pi-Flow timestep distillation to reach 25.1x with GenEval 0.85. - The missing: All quantitative results are single-GPU (A100) measurements on two backbones (FLUX.1-dev, Qwen-Image) with no human preference study or failure-case rate reported, so degradation on prompts requiring fine small-scale text/texture at the low-resolution stage is only shown qualitatively (Figure 3), not quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone deploying flow-matching text-to-image inference who needs wall-clock speedup without retraining; the frequency-domain argument for why low-strength noise injection works (Appendix C.3) is the part most transferable to other coarse-to-fine pipelines.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (66) and Non-Obviousness (70) drive the composite: each of the three design choices (pixel-space upsampling, GAN-class SR, calibrated noise) is backed by a targeted ablation, and the frequency-domain SNR argument for why low-strength noise injection repairs only the SR error band is a genuinely non-obvious insight, not a predictable combination. Novelty (65) reflects a new method plus a formulation with an accompanying noise-level lower bound. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are solid but bounded by the lack of any human-preference study and no reported failure-rate analysis, so all quality claims rest on automated proxies (GenEval/DPG/OneIG). Practical Impact (60) is real (training-free, composes with distillation to 25x) but no code release is mentioned. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*66 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*60 = 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 66 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-to-image diffusion/flow-matching models (FLUX.1-dev, Qwen-Image-20B) cost up to 47 seconds per image at 1024x1024 on an A100, which is prohibitive for interactive or high-throughput use. Hardware-agnostic acceleration strategies exist: timestep distillation (requires fine-tuning, non-negligible training cost), feature caching (training-free but capped near 4x speedup), token pruning (under 1.5x), and multi-resolution generation (training-free, over 5x, but this last class performs its upsampling inside the latent space and either relies on runtime dynamic region-identification or exhibits blurring/artifacts from destroying local latent spatial statistics). The gap MrFlow targets is a multi-resolution scheme that avoids both the artifact problem of latent-space upsampling and the runtime-statistics dependency of prior methods, while pushing past the roughly 5x-8x ceiling where existing training-free methods (RALU, SPEED, TeaCache, DB-Taylor) visibly collapse. Key Idea: Perform the resolution upsampling step in pixel space using a pretrained GAN super-resolution network rather than in the latent space, then re-encode through the VAE and inject only enough Gaussian noise to raise the high-frequency SNR at the level the SR output actually introduced errors. Because the VAE encoder attenuates out-of-distribution high-frequency components and the GAN SR error is empirically shown to be almost entirely a high-frequency-band defect (Table 9's kNN-distance-in-DINOv2 ablation), a small, calibrated noise injection (sigma in [0.1, 0.15]) lets a single high-resolution flow step resample and repair just that band, without disturbing the already-correct low-frequency structure or requiring many additional steps. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages, each with an explicit design decision: (1) Low-resolution structure generation: sample z1 ~ N(0,I) in a small latent grid, run K_L=12 Euler steps of the pretrained rectified-flow ODE (z_{t-h} = z_t - hv_theta(z_t,t,c)), decode via the pretrained VAE decoder to pixel space. (2) Pixel-space super-resolution: apply a pretrained Real-ESRGAN (GAN-based) upsampler U to the low-resolution decoded image, chosen over interpolation or L2-trained SwinIR/OSEDiff because GAN loss produces sharp, natural-image-like high-frequency content whose bias is confined to the high-frequency band (versus diffuse blur from L2 methods), which the refine stage can later correct. (3) Low-strength noise injection: re-encode the SR pixel output through the VAE encoder to get z_0^SR, then form z_t^HR = (1-sigma_t)z_0^SR + sigma_tepsilon with sigma_t in [0.1,0.15]; the paper derives a lower bound sigma_t >= sqrt(lambda_hf)/(1+sqrt(lambda_hf)) from an SNR condition on the measured high-frequency power lambda_hf of the clean latent (Appendix C.3, using the Tweedie/MMSE posterior-mean equivalence). (4) High-resolution refinement: run K_H=1 Euler step of the same flow velocity network at high resolution starting from z_t^HR, then decode. The three critical design decisions -- pixel-space (not latent-space) upsampling, GAN-class (not L2 regression) SR, and noise level calibrated to the SR error's frequency band -- are each backed by a targeted ablation (Table 3, Table 9, Appendix C.2-C.3) rather than asserted without evidence, which is a genuine methodological strength. Key Results: On FLUX.1-dev at 1024x1024, MrFlow (20 low-res + 1 high-res steps) reaches 5.78x speedup with GenEval 0.65 versus native 0.66 (50 steps, 1x); at the more aggressive 12+1 configuration, 8.25x speedup with GenEval 0.63. On Qwen-Image, the corresponding configurations give 6.98x speedup (GenEval 0.87 vs native 0.88) and 10.3x speedup (GenEval 0.86, DPG 87.10 vs native 88.67, OneIG-En/Zh 0.52/0.51 vs native 0.53/0.53) -- roughly a 1-2% metric gap at 10x speedup. Baselines compared: ToMA (token merging, only 1.05-1.13x, collapses visually despite flat metrics), TeaCache and DB-Taylor (feature caching, hold up to about 4-5x then collapse by 9x, e.g. DB-Taylor GenEval drops to 0.09 at 9.34x on Qwen-Image), RALU and SPEED (prior multi-resolution methods, degrade earlier and more visibly than MrFlow at matched speedups). Against training-dependent timestep-distillation methods (SenseFlow, Pi-Flow, FLUX-schnell), MrFlow's training-free 12+1 configuration is close to 4-step SenseFlow's accuracy at similar speedup, and MrFlow composed with Pi-Flow (denoted MrFlow-dagger) reaches 25.1x speedup on Qwen-Image with GenEval 0.85 (vs native 0.88) and OneIG loss under 1%. All experiments run on a single Nvidia A100 GPU; the paper reports end-to-end wall-clock speedup (including VAE encode/decode, SR, text encoding), which is a fairer comparison basis than the latent-diffusion-only timings some baselines report in their original papers. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the staged low-to-high pipeline with pixel-space GAN SR plus calibrated noise injection) combined with a NEW FORMULATION for how much noise to inject, backed by an accompanying theoretical derivation (a lower bound on noise level from the SR error's high-frequency power). The individual components (rectified flow, GAN super-resolution, SDEdit-style noise-then-denoise) are all established techniques; the genuine novelty is the specific frequency-domain argument for why pixel-space SR followed by low-strength noise avoids the artifacts that plague latent-space upsampling, and the empirical demonstration that a single high-resolution step suffices for refinement. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes substantive ablations: step-configuration sweeps (10/12/16/20 low-res x 1/2/3 high-res steps, Figure 5), SR-method comparison (Table 3: interpolation, SwinIR, OSEDiff, Real-ESRGAN), and a targeted noise-injection ablation replacing Gaussian noise with structured (high-freq, low-freq, channel-correlated) noise to isolate which frequency band the refine step can correct (Table 9). Results are averaged over 3 prompts x 3-4 seeds for the analytical tables (Tables 6-9), though the main Table 1/2 comparison tables do not state the number of seeds or report variance/error bars, and no significance testing is reported anywhere. Code, weights, and data availability are not stated in the excerpted text. Evaluation uses three independent public benchmarks (GenEval, DPG-Bench, OneIG-Bench) rather than a self-constructed metric, which mitigates Goodhart's Law concerns, and comparisons load baseline methods' official pretrained weights without retraining, avoiding a common self-serving-baseline pitfall. This is a preprint (arXiv, submitted July 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a training-free inference-time strategy evaluated purely by generation quality and measured wall-clock speedup, no formal proofs of correctness). Practical: Yes, runs on a single commodity Nvidia A100 with no custom kernels, hardware, or runtime dynamic identification of image regions. Efficient: Adds one VAE encode/decode round-trip and one Real-ESRGAN forward pass per image versus native inference, which the paper states is small relative to full diffusion cost; the low-resolution stage itself gives roughly 4x per-step savings from token-count reduction. Effective: Automated metrics (GenEval, DPG, OneIG) plus qualitative comparisons (Figures 1, 3, 4) support the claim, though no human-preference study is reported, so "effective" rests entirely on automated benchmark proxies. Scalable: Not tested at resolutions or model sizes beyond the two backbones and 1024x1024 (with brief mentions of 1328x1328/1584x1056 and newer models FLUX.2/Z-Image in Appendix B); the paper does not report what happens at, say, 4K generation or on 100B+-parameter models, so scalability beyond the tested regime is unverified. The work delivers a training-free inference procedure applicable to any pretrained flow-matching checkpoint but no released code repository is mentioned in the excerpted sections. Strengths: - Grounds each of the three key design choices (pixel-space upsampling, GAN-class SR, calibrated noise level) in a dedicated ablation rather than intuition alone, including a formal SNR-based lower bound for the noise level derived from measured high-frequency power (Appendix C.3). - Demonstrates orthogonal composability with timestep distillation (Pi-Flow, FLUX-schnell) without additional training, reaching 25.1x speedup, which is a genuinely useful practical property beyond the standalone training-free result. - Uses end-to-end wall-clock speedup measurement (including VAE, SR, text encoding) rather than latent-diffusion-only timing, which the paper explicitly notes gives fairer, sometimes less favorable, comparisons than baselines' self-reported numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper states that additional high-resolution refinement steps beyond one bring only marginal changes under the low-strength noising regime (Section 3.4), implicitly acknowledging the method's benefit is concentrated in a narrow noise/step regime. It also notes that SR-choice results are close on automatic metrics despite visible differences under visual inspection (Table 3 vs. Figure 3), an honest admission that the chosen automated metrics can miss real quality differences the paper itself relies on elsewhere. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No human evaluation or user study is reported; all quality claims rest on GenEval/DPG/OneIG plus the authors' own visual selections, which is a narrower evidence base than the paper's confident framing suggests. The core noise-level heuristic (sigma in [0.1, 0.15]) is derived from and validated on natural-image high-frequency power statistics measured on the same two backbones used for the main results; no evidence is given that this range transfers to different VAE architectures, aspect ratios, or domains (e.g., sparse-line art, dense text-heavy images) where the paper's own Figure 3 shows character-stroke and small-text artifacts are the failure mode most sensitive to SR choice. The comparison to timestep-distillation baselines (SenseFlow, Pi-Flow) uses only their released 4-step configuration; whether MrFlow's training-free approach would still be competitive against a distillation model retrained specifically to compete at MrFlow's exact FLOP budget is not tested. Failure-rate or worst-case analysis (e.g., percentage of prompts where the low-resolution stage locks in an incorrect global structure that the single high-resolution step cannot fix) is not reported, only aggregate benchmark scores. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying pretrained flow-matching text-to-image models for latency-sensitive inference gains a concrete, ablation-supported staged-sampling recipe that measurably beats other training-free accelerators at matched speedup and composes with distillation, making it worth adopting over feature-caching or token-pruning alternatives in the 5x-25x speedup range.
Recommended 2607.01642 PDF
#22 — Innovative (65)

Deciphering Fingerprints of 3D Molecular Surfaces for Accurate Epitope Prediction

F Wu, W Xuan, J Leskovec, Y Choi, LE Li · arxiv.org · Jure Leskovec - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SurfBind, a surface-centric Transformer framework (SurfFormer++) for antibody-specific B-cell epitope (BCE) prediction that combines patch-level surface pretraining, binder-aware cross-attention, and coarse-to-fine point/patch prediction. - Why it's important: Roughly 90% of native B-cell epitopes are conformational and discontinuous in sequence, and existing sequence- or backbone-based predictors struggle to localize antibody-specific binding sites, which matters for antibody engineering, immunotherapy, and vaccine design. - How it does it: It partitions the antigen surface point cloud into geodesically local patches, encodes them with a vector-quantized masked-autoencoder pretraining scheme with geometric and physicochemical decoding targets, then uses cross-attention between antibody and antigen patch features to make the prediction binder-aware. - The good: On SAbDab-test, SurfBind reaches AUC-PR 0.305 and F1 0.429, beating the next-best structure-based method (DiscoTope-3.0, AUC-PR 0.207/F1 0.386) and clearing AF-Multimer (AUC-ROC 0.698 vs. SurfBind's 0.816) despite AF-Multimer's strong general PPI folding. - The missing: No hypothesis testing or variance/seed reporting is done anywhere in the results (explicitly acknowledged only for the docking experiment), so the margin over DiscoTope-3.0 (AUC-PR 0.305 vs. 0.207) is not accompanied by any significance measure. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on antibody-antigen interface prediction or surface-based geometric deep learning; the core surface-pretraining recipe (VQ + physicochemical decoding targets) is the reusable part, while the absolute numbers should be read with the missing significance testing in mind.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The composite is held below 75 primarily by Rigor (62): informative ablations (SurfFormer 0.768 to VQ 0.816, chemical pretext +0.033 AUC-ROC) and methodologically sound sequence-identity-clustered splits, but no error bars, seeds, or significance tests for any headline number and no code/weight release. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (68) are solid for binder-aware cross-attention plus a surface-native VQ-MAE recipe, a real conceptual improvement over partner-agnostic predictors. Impact (62) is helped by a genuine downstream docking validation (DockQ 26.1% to 38.0%) but capped by a modest absolute AUC-PR (0.306) and no release. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*62 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Antibody-antigen binding is mediated by epitopes, localized antigen surface regions whose 3D geometry and physicochemistry determine binding specificity; identifying these regions correctly is central to antibody engineering, immunotherapy, and epitope-based vaccine design. Roughly 90% of native B-cell epitopes are conformational (discontinuous in sequence but spatially co-located in 3D), so sequence-based predictors that treat epitopes as contiguous residue runs, and backbone-only structural predictors, both struggle to resolve fine-grained, partner-dependent binding patterns. A further complication is that epitope prediction is inherently antibody-context-dependent: the same antigen surface exposes different binding regions depending on which antibody it faces, so partner-agnostic predictions (the dominant existing paradigm, per the paper's own baseline categorization) are unreliable for antibody-specific discovery. Key Idea: Treat the molecular surface, not the sequence or backbone graph, as the primary modeling substrate, and make the model binder-aware by cross-attending antigen surface patches against antibody patch (or PLM-derived) representations, rather than predicting a single partner-agnostic epitope map per antigen. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Surface generation: the antigen is converted to an oriented point cloud via stochastic SDF sampling, and per-point features are extracted with a quasi-geodesic-convolution point cloud network (residue-level chemical types plus local geometric frames). (2) Patch partition and ordering: farthest-point sampling picks patch centers, KNN forms patches, and Morton (Z-order) coding imposes a sequence structure over the otherwise unordered patch set to enable Transformer-style processing. (3) SurfFormer++ backbone: patch embeddings undergo multi-head self-attention with geodesic-distance-based relative positional encodings (invariant to rotation/translation) plus absolute sinusoidal positional embeddings from the Morton ordering, and a binder-aware cross-attention block exchanges information between antibody (ligand) and antigen (receptor) patches, with antibody representations obtained from either structure encoders or protein language models (ESM-2) when no antibody structure is available. (4) Coarse-to-fine prediction: patch-level features are pooled to coarse logits, then propagated back to individual surface points via inverse-geodesic-distance-weighted interpolation (a PointNet++-style operation) for fine-grained point-level logits, trained with a weighted sum of coarse (patch) and fine (point) binary cross-entropy losses using soft labels for patches.
