Methodology: The pipeline has three asynchronous, HuggingFace-Hub-mediated components (a training worker, N parallel Isaac Sim rollout workers, and a manual DAgger station) with no synchronization barriers. Reward is a keypoint-distance-based binary success signal densified into per-garment intermediate checkpoints (0.5 reward for a mid-fold checkpoint, 1.0 for full success), with failure withdrawing all accumulated reward smoothed across the last frames rather than as one spike. Advantage combines a success-probability value baseline (dampened by a fixed coefficient alpha_s=0.5, motivated by an approximate CUPED-style correction) and a policy-stable completion-prediction potential-shaping term, aggregated via GAE (gamma=0.999, lambda=0.99) computed offline before each training iteration; stale rollouts are handled by decaying dataset sampling share (0.98/iteration) and blending toward a GRPO-style outcome-only baseline as predictions go stale. At inference, execution length, playback speed, inpainting onset, classifier-free guidance scale, noise temperature, and best-of-N candidate count are each tuned per garment type via an independent per-parameter Thompson-sampling bandit run online during rollout collection, then frozen for submission. The three critical design decisions are: (1) using AWR through the sampler rather than loss-weighting, argued (not proven) to keep effective batch utilization at 100%; (2) dampening rather than fully subtracting the value baseline, justified by an approximate bias-variance argument but with the optimal coefficient theta acknowledged as not computable in practice; (3) the checkpoint-rollback training trick (periodically reverting to an earlier checkpoint and retraining on all data collected since), justified by analogy to Physical Intelligence's pi_0.6 but applied "less methodically" (done ad hoc, 3-4 times total) rather than systematically. Key Results: Online round (simulation): 79.63% overall success across 80 held-out garments (20 each of four types, 10 seen + 10 unseen), 1st of 62 teams, 6.1 percentage points ahead of 2nd place (73.50%). Per-type scores: 74.5% (long top), 70.0% (short top), 80.5% (long pants), 93.5% (short pants). Real-world final: 2nd place of teams evaluated on-site, 865 points against 1st place's 895 (out of a possible 1080, on a composite success-rate-plus-quality metric, not raw success percentage). Baselines are the other 61 competing teams' independently built systems rather than a controlled ablation baseline, so the comparison establishes competitive ranking but not the marginal value of any specific component. Data scale: approximately 12,500 simulated rollout episodes (~4.3M frames) across ~140 collection sessions for the sim round, and 1,292 real-robot episodes (~638K frames, combining organizer data, home teleop, home DAgger) for the one-week sim-to-real fine-tuning sprint. The garment-type classifier reaches >99% accuracy late in training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the AWR+RECAP combination for flow-matching VLAs, and the single-model-as-its-own-value-function architecture) and ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (the asynchronous training/rollout/DAgger flywheel, Thompson-sampling inference-parameter tuning, sim-to-real augmentation recipe). The paper itself is explicit that it "mostly recombines existing RL ideas" (AWR, RECAP, GAE, DAgger, classifier-free guidance, Thompson sampling) with engineering contributions layered on top; the genuinely new element is the specific combination and the shared-network value/Q design, not any individual algorithmic primitive. Rigor & Reproducibility: The author states directly and repeatedly that no proper ablation study was performed ('I present the architecture as is... a real ablation study would multiply the budget') for the model architecture, and design decisions in the reward/advantage pipeline are similarly not ablated in isolation; there are no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests anywhere, and this is a single-run, single-team competition entry, not a controlled experiment, which the author flags explicitly in the abstract ('This report is an engineering case study, not a controlled experiment'). Code, both final checkpoints (sim-round and real-world-final), and a companion blog post with video examples are released at named GitHub and HuggingFace Hub URLs, which is a genuine reproducibility positive. The competition itself is a strong evaluation vehicle (independently run, 62 competing teams, held-out unseen garments, a real-robot final round the author had no access to during development), functioning as an external, adversarial benchmark rather than a self-constructed one, which mitigates (but does not eliminate) the lack of internal ablations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built training/inference pipeline evaluated by competition deployment on both simulated and real hardware). Practical: Yes for training (1x H200 plus RTX PRO 6000 GPUs for rollout collection, consumer-adjacent hardware) and yes for real-world deployment (a bimanual SO-ARM101 rig plus a workstation-class GPU for inference). Efficient: Training compute (~300K steps, batch size 192, on one H200) is modest relative to large-scale RL and the real-round fine-tune took roughly one week, though sim-side data collection (12,500 episodes, ~4.3M frames) represents substantial GPU-hour cost the paper does not quantify in dollars or GPU-hours. Effective: Yes on the stated problem (competition success metric) but the paper itself flags that the policy overfits to sim-specific rendering artifacts (demonstrated via a resize-order ablation that revealed near-perfect discrimination between differently-preprocessed but visually identical images) and that dexterity/precision failures (near-miss keypoint distances) remain the dominant failure mode, so effectiveness is qualified rather than complete. Scalable: Not tested; the paper explicitly frames its own scale as "modest by large-scale-RL standards" and states a smaller model would likely suffice for this task, but no experiment varies model size or data scale to test this claim. The work delivers two released trained checkpoints, full training/inference code, and a companion tooling utility (camera-overlay alignment) as reusable software artifacts. Strengths: - Combining the policy's own auxiliary heads into a shared value/Q function is a real architectural insight that removes an entire separate critic network from the training pipeline while giving completion, keypoint-distance, and best-of-N scoring signals essentially for free from one forward pass. - The reward-shaping mechanics (gradual first-checkpoint allocation, uniform failure withdrawal, CUPED-inspired value dampening, EMA smoothing with an offline value-tail correction for near-miss frames) are genuinely careful engineering that directly targets known pathologies of naive potential-based shaping under a noisy value predictor. - The external validation is strong: 1st of 62 independently competing teams by a 6.1-point margin on held-out unseen garments is a much harder-to-game outcome than a self-reported ablation table. - The sim-to-real recipe (camera-overlay alignment tool, per-source motion-intensity resampling, three-bucket training mix with explicit down-weighting of mismatched-rig data) is a concrete, transferable checklist for anyone doing sim-to-real robotics with a similar mismatch-to-eval-rig problem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The author confronts limitations directly and extensively (Section 10 is largely a limitations discussion). Explicitly stated: no proper ablations were run for architecture choices or reward design; the model overfit to sim-specific rendering details (demonstrated with a concrete resize-order test); the policy has essentially no recovery/exploration capability, so it fails outright once out-of-distribution rather than self-correcting; the units-conversion bug (LeRobot state-representation change) cost real-robot data quality and was only caught two days before the deadline; DAgger data collection focused on hard initial states that turned out to be less representative of the actual evaluation distribution than expected, a strategic misallocation the author states directly ('I suspect I would have scored higher had I instead concentrated on polishing clean completions'); and the two competition rounds (RL-heavy simulation, DAgger-heavy real-world) were solved with two largely separate toolkits rather than one unified pipeline, which the author calls the biggest regret. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper's advantage-computation and dampening coefficients (alpha_s=0.5, various loss weights in Table 3, EMA coefficients) appear to be manually tuned to this specific competition and are not shown to transfer to other manipulation tasks; there is no comparison against a simpler baseline (e.g., BC alone, or PPO/GRPO directly adapted to flow-matching) that would isolate how much of the 79.63% result is attributable to the AWR+RECAP combination specifically versus the substantial engineering investment in reward shaping, augmentation, and inference-time tuning applied on top of any RL method. The claim that '2-3 candidate rollouts consistently beat single-candidate ones' for best-of-N selection is reported despite the paper's own finding that the correlation between the scoring head's prediction and actual outcome was 'effectively zero' in the author's experiments, an internal tension the paper notes but does not resolve. The real-world evaluation (2nd place, 5 garments per type, small sample) is a much lower-N test than the simulation round (80 garments), so the sim-to-real generalization claim rests on a thinner evidence base than the headline simulation result. Bottom Line: A practitioner building RL-on-VLA pipelines for deformable-object manipulation gains a concrete, competition-validated recipe (reward shaping, AWR+RECAP combination, Thompson-sampling inference tuning, sim-to-real augmentation checklist) even though the paper's own admission of zero controlled ablations means no single component's marginal contribution can be trusted independent of the whole system.
Recommended 2606.27163 PDF
#34 — Innovative (62)

DiLaServe: High SLO Attainment Serving for Diffusion Language Models

TT Chang, BY Hong, K Pham, S Venkataraman · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DiLaServe, a cluster-level serving system for diffusion language models (DLMs) that jointly adjusts per-request confidence thresholds and cluster tensor-parallelism configuration via ILP-based reconfiguration to meet latency SLOs. - Why it's important: DLMs offer up to 1.75x higher throughput than autoregressive models at comparable quality, but no existing serving system exploits their unique confidence-based speed-quality knob or handles the load heterogeneity introduced by approximate KV caching, leaving that throughput advantage unrealized under SLO constraints. - How it does it: A Step Predictor (gradient boosting) estimates remaining denoising steps per request, feeding an SLO-aware confidence-threshold selection algorithm plus a cluster-wide adaptive load-control cap, while a two-stage ILP periodically re-derives the number of TP-degree instances and a minimal-migration transition plan. - The good: On a real-world ShareGPT/BurstGPT-derived trace, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by 21.1-30.2 percentage points over adapted INFaaS and Llumnix baselines with only a 0.09-point quality-score drop (LLM-judge scale 0-10). - The missing: All baselines (INFaaS, Llumnix) are autoregressive-serving systems retrofitted for DLMs by the authors themselves, so there is no comparison against any other purpose-built DLM serving system or against a strong non-ILP heuristic scheduler. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or operating LLM/DLM serving infrastructure, especially teams already running vLLM/Ray clusters; primarily a systems contribution, not directly relevant to DLM model or training research.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (72) reflects treating the confidence threshold as a first-class schedulable resource with no autoregressive analogue, plus a two-stage ILP incorporating KV-cache step heterogeneity. Practical Impact (60) is a ~9,500-LOC system on vLLM/Ray with 21-30 point SLO gains, but no release and untested beyond 16 GPUs despite billions-of-queries motivation. Rigor (55) is pulled down because all baselines are the authors' own retrofits of autoregressive systems (no DLM-native comparison), no seeds, no error bars, and the LLM-judge quality metric is uncalibrated. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are helped by direct confrontation of the batch-size and homogeneous-SLO simplifications. Weighted: 0.15*72+0.15*55+0.10*65+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*60 = 62.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion language models generate multiple tokens per denoising step via confidence-thresholded unmasking, giving them throughput advantages over autoregressive models, but service providers deploying them at scale (billions of queries per day) must simultaneously meet strict latency SLOs. Doing so requires navigating three DLM-specific difficulties that autoregressive-serving systems were never built to handle: a per-step confidence threshold that trades quality for step count, a tensor-parallelism degree choice that trades per-request latency for aggregate throughput, and approximate KV-caching mechanisms that make step costs non-uniform (cache steps vs. recompute steps). Prior LLM-serving optimizations (INFaaS, Llumnix, and others) target autoregressive models and do not expose or exploit the confidence-threshold knob, nor do they reason about DLM-specific caching heterogeneity. Key Idea: Treat the confidence threshold as a first-class, dynamically adjustable control variable that couples individual-request deadline management with cluster-wide load control and TP-degree provisioning, solved jointly through a hierarchical ILP rather than treating threshold selection, scheduling, and cluster sizing as separate, independently-tuned subsystems. Methodology: The pipeline has four coupled components. (1) A Step Predictor, a gradient boosting machine trained offline on 100 profiled requests per model, maps request state (denoising progress, output length, token-confidence percentiles) to predicted remaining denoising steps per candidate threshold; updates are batched at coarse "granularity" (grouped token-unmasking events) rather than per-token, cutting execution overhead from 20% to under 5% relative error increase. (2) SLO-aware Confidence Threshold Adjustment (Algorithm 1) picks, per request, the highest threshold from a discrete candidate set whose predicted remaining latency (steps x per-step latency from a profiled latency table) still fits the SLO budget. (3) Adaptive Load Control (Algorithm 2) bounds the maximum threshold any request may claim by conservatively estimating worst-case aggregate cluster load (assuming all pending requests use the lowest threshold) against total cluster capacity, iterating requests in arrival order. (4) A two-stage integer linear program (solved with Gurobi) periodically (e.g., every 5 minutes) first maximizes average confidence threshold subject to per-class SLO and system-stability (queueing) constraints to determine the target number of TP-d instances, then computes a minimal-disruption reconfiguration plan that maximizes preserved (non-migrated) instances. Migration is restricted to KV-cache recompute steps to avoid rebuild overhead. Design decisions: restricting migration to recompute-step boundaries is empirically justified (Table 6: unrestricted migration costs 17-38% extra latency vs. recompute-only); the choice of coarse-granularity step prediction is justified by Figure 7's overhead/accuracy sweep; the two-stage ILP separation (capacity planning then migration-minimizing plan) is reasonable engineering but not proven optimal jointly, since sequential optimization of two objectives can be strictly suboptimal versus a single joint ILP. Key Results: On a 16-GPU cluster (GH200/H100 nodes) serving LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B over a ShareGPT-prompt/BurstGPT-arrival synthetic trace (63,736 requests), DiLaServe achieves 91.13% SLO attainment vs. 60.98% (Llumnix) and 70.03% (INFaaS), with average end-to-end latency of 5.75s vs. 10.64s and 8.80s respectively, and LLM-judge quality scores of 6.80 vs. 6.89 and 6.89 (a 0.09-point drop). Across three accuracy benchmarks (GSM8K math, MMLU-Pro general, MBPP coding) with LLaDA and Dream under varying request rates and SLO multiples, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points over Llumnix and up to 55 points over INFaaS at high load, with accuracy drops of at most 0.7-0.9%. Ablations show SLO-aware threshold adjustment alone contributes up to 70.8 points of SLO-attainment improvement over a fixed threshold, and adaptive load control adds up to 42.4 further points, at accuracy cost of at most 1.7-1.9%. Baselines are the authors' own DLM adaptations of established autoregressive systems (INFaaS, Llumnix), not independently validated DLM-serving baselines, so "strong" is relative to systems not designed for this workload. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL and NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel elements are the joint per-request confidence-threshold control coupled with cluster-wide adaptive load control, and the two-stage ILP formulation that incorporates KV-cache step heterogeneity into reconfiguration; the underlying building blocks (ILP-based cluster sizing, gradient-boosted latency prediction, migration-based load balancing) are standard techniques from prior LLM-serving systems (Llumnix, INFaaS, DynamoLLM) applied to the new DLM setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes substantial ablations isolating the contribution of each mechanism (threshold control, load control, migration granularity, KV-caching support) and evaluates across three benchmarks, two models, and two clusters, which is a genuine strength. However: no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed repetitions are reported for any of the throughput/latency/SLO numbers, so the statistical significance of reported gaps (e.g., 0.09-point quality difference) is not established. The LLM-as-judge quality metric (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) is not validated against human judgments or calibrated for consistency, a known reliability concern for LLM-judge evaluations. No code, data, or artifact release URL is stated in the paper. Baselines are the authors' own re-implementations/adaptations of INFaaS and Llumnix for DLM serving rather than the original systems or independently DLM-native competitors, which raises a fairness question the paper does not address (no comparison against, e.g., a naive DLM serving system with static confidence and no reconfiguration, beyond what's implied in ablations). Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built serving system evaluated by deployment on real GPU clusters). Practical: Yes, built on vLLM and Ray and evaluated on commodity 16-GPU H100/GH200 clusters, using an off-the-shelf commercial ILP solver (Gurobi). Efficient: The system adds ~9,500 lines of Python on top of vLLM; ILP solves run periodically (every 5 minutes) rather than per-request, and step prediction overhead is kept under 5% relative error increase via batched, coarse-granularity updates, but the paper does not report Gurobi solve times or the ILP's own scalability limits as cluster size or request-class count (|K|) grows. Effective: Evidence is strong within the tested setting: real-world trace and three accuracy benchmarks show large SLO-attainment gains with minimal quality loss. Scalable: Untested beyond a 16-GPU cluster; the paper does not report results at 160 or 1,600 GPUs, and the ILP solve time as a function of node count, TP-degree options, and request classes is unstated, so scalability to industry-scale clusters (the paper's own motivating context of billions of queries per day) is only claimed, not demonstrated. The work delivers a software artifact (a system built on vLLM/Ray) but does not state whether the implementation is or will be open-sourced. Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a genuinely DLM-specific serving problem (confidence threshold as a schedulable resource) that has no analogue in autoregressive serving, rather than repackaging generic ML-serving techniques. - The adaptive load control mechanism (Algorithm 2) is a well-motivated worst-case aggregate-load bound that provably keeps utilization within cluster capacity while preserving per-request flexibility, evaluated with a clear ablation showing its distinct contribution (up to 42.4 points). - Careful empirical characterization of KV-cache step heterogeneity (cache vs. recompute steps) and its integration into both load estimation and migration policy, backed by a targeted micro-experiment (Table 6) showing why migration must be restricted to recompute steps. - Extensive ablation coverage (confidence-threshold control, migration granularity, caching on/off, input/output length sweeps) that isolates the contribution of each design choice rather than presenting only end-to-end numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly notes the batch-size selection constraint in the ILP formulation ("all model instances of a given TP degree share a single batch size configuration... a modeling simplification that keeps the optimization problem manageable") is not a fundamental system requirement but a tractability trade-off. It also acknowledges the homogeneous-SLO assumption in the main design and dedicates an extensions section (3.5) to sketching (without evaluating) non-homogeneous SLOs and request-priority support. The paper confronts these scope limits directly rather than hiding them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison against any DLM-native serving system beyond the authors' own adaptations of autoregressive baselines; a system billed as "the first cluster-level serving system designed specifically for DLMs" (per the authors' own claim) cannot be benchmarked against a prior DLM-specific baseline, which is understandable given novelty but leaves open whether a simpler DLM-aware heuristic (e.g., fixed-threshold-per-load-tier without full ILP reconfiguration) would close most of the gap at far lower engineering complexity. - The extension sections for non-homogeneous SLOs and request priority (3.5) are proposed but never empirically evaluated, so their claimed "minimal modification" compatibility is asserted, not demonstrated. - Reconfiguration overhead itself (time to solve the two-stage ILP, plus instance launch/terminate latency) is not quantified in the main results, only its downstream effect on SLO attainment; a reader cannot assess whether reconfiguration decisions themselves introduce transient service degradation windows. - Step Predictor training uses only 100 profiled requests per model per candidate threshold; no discussion of how this predictor degrades under distribution shift (e.g., a new workload with very different input/output length distributions than ShareGPT/BurstGPT) or whether retraining is needed per deployment. Bottom Line: A practitioner operating LLM/DLM serving infrastructure gains a concrete, empirically-validated blueprint for exploiting DLM-specific confidence-threshold control jointly with cluster reconfiguration, and the ablations make the individual mechanism contributions legible, but the lack of any DLM-native baseline comparison and untested scalability beyond 16 GPUs mean the headline 30-56 percentage-point SLO gains should be read as gains over naively-adapted autoregressive systems rather than over the best plausible DLM-aware alternative.
Recommended 2606.29094 PDF
#35 — Innovative (61)

DualEval: Joint Model-Item Calibration for Unified LLM Evaluation

AJ Li, H Huang, Y Park, Y Ma, WL Chiang, L Chen… · arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DualEval, a joint item-response-theory (IRT) framework that places model abilities and item (question) parameters from static benchmarks and arena-style preference data on a single shared latent scale, fitting both signals simultaneously. - Why it's important: LLM leaderboards mix static benchmarks (which saturate and get contaminated) with arena preference data (which is subjective and model-centric); no prior framework unifies both into one calibrated, item-aware scale that also tells you which questions actually carry ranking signal. - How it does it: A two-parameter logistic IRT model fits static correctness labels while a reward-distilled soft pairwise Bradley-Terry-style loss fits arena preference gaps, and both share the same latent ability/difficulty/sharpness parameters (theta, b, k) updated jointly under one regularized objective. - The good: The Fisher-information-based subset-recovery experiment shows the top 10% of items by informativeness reproduce the full 18-model ranking at Spearman rho >= 0.95 across all three static-anchored domains, a concrete, actionable diagnostic for benchmark compression. - The missing: The entire arena supervision signal depends on one proprietary, non-reproducible reward model for the primary results; the public-RM replication only reaches rho 0.835 agreement in the miscellaneous domain, and no experiment substitutes real human preference labels for reward-model scores at the scale used in the main results. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or auditing LLM leaderboards or benchmark-compression pipelines; the IRT formalism and Fisher-information diagnostic are reusable even without adopting the specific reward model.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (68) is the main positive — a NEW FORMULATION placing static IRT and reward-distilled pairwise IRT on one shared latent scale, with a well-ablated both-bad anchoring mechanism (BB-AUC 0.903 vs 0.622) and a Fisher-information subset-recovery diagnostic (top 10% items recover full ranking at rho >= 0.95). Rigor (58) and Resilience (58) pull the composite down: the entire arena branch depends on a single proprietary, non-reproducible reward model, no code/data/weights are released, each model-item pair uses one response with no resampling, and an unvalidated LLM-judge domain filter gates the arena prompts. The public-RM replication reaching only rho 0.835 in the miscellaneous domain and synthetic-only contamination injection further limit how far the numbers transfer. Weighted composite lands at 61 — a clear contribution whose reproducibility and supervision-source weaknesses keep it below the top tier.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM evaluation pipelines rely on two disconnected signal types: static benchmarks with binary correctness labels (which saturate as models improve and are vulnerable to contamination) and arena-style pairwise preference comparisons (which are open-ended but noisy, subjective, and vulnerable to annotator/reward-model bias). Both settings are model-centric: they aggregate item-level outcomes into a single model score while treating every question as equally informative, even though items where nearly all models pass or fail carry almost no discriminative signal. Practitioners running leaderboards (e.g., Chatbot Arena style operators, benchmark maintainers) lack a principled way to (a) combine the two supervision sources into one comparable ranking and (b) diagnose which subset of items is actually load-bearing for that ranking, or whether observed (model, item) outcomes are anomalous (contamination, leakage, data-quality issues). Key Idea: Represent both static correctness and arena preference outcomes as functions of the same latent ability-minus-difficulty gap (theta_i - b_q), so that a single joint optimization over (theta, b, k) lets static labels and preference comparisons mutually constrain the same underlying scale; this converts previously separate score aggregations into one calibrated measurement instrument whose item parameters (difficulty, sharpness, Fisher information) become directly interpretable and reusable for diagnostics. Methodology: The pipeline has two supervision branches feeding one shared parameter set. (1) Static IRT: a standard two-parameter logistic model p_{i,q} = sigmoid(a_q(theta_i - b_q)) is fit via binary cross-entropy against observed correctness labels, with sharpness a_q = exp(k_q). (2) Reward-distilled pairwise IRT: for arena prompts, a reward model scores each response; rewards are z-standardized per (domain, RM), converted into soft pairwise preference targets p_{ijq} = sigmoid(z_{i,q} - z_{j,q}), and fit via a Bradley-Terry-style loss over the same theta/b parameters with a learned arena-temperature gamma. Both-bad (both models scored below a percentile threshold) and tie-filtering heuristics remove uninformative or ambiguous pairs before loss computation. The joint objective L = lambda_static L_static + lambda_arena * L_arena plus L2 regularization on theta, b, k, and log(gamma/gamma_0) is optimized with Adam (lr 0.02, 2000 epochs), with model abilities re-centered to zero mean after each step for identifiability. Critical design decisions: (i) sharing (theta, b, k) across both loss branches rather than fitting them separately is the core mechanism enabling cross-source calibration, well-justified by the ablation showing static-only and arena-only baselines each transfer weakly to the other's reference ranking; (ii) both-bad anchoring in the arena loss is empirically justified (ablation: BB-AUC drops from 0.903 to 0.622 without it); (iii) using a single learned scalar reward model as the sole arena supervision source is a design choice with less justification, since it hinges on a proprietary, non-public model whose robustness is checked with only one public alternative (Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B). Key Results: Evaluated on 18 frontier LLMs (GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4, DeepSeek-V3.2, etc.) across four domains (coding: 600 items, math: 400, misc: 600, generic arena-only: 500). DualEval reconstructs both signal types with 88-92% binary static-label accuracy and 68-81% arena decisive-pair accuracy. Compared with static-only 2PL and arena-only Bradley-Terry baselines (Table 2), DualEval achieves static rho 0.959-0.990 (near static-only's 1.0 reference) while improving arena rho to 0.734-0.878 versus static-only's 0.571-0.773, without matching arena-only's rho ~1.0 on its own reference (an expected trade-off since arena-only ignores static labels entirely). Under reward-model substitution (public Skywork RM), arena-only Bradley-Terry rankings degrade sharply against the public arena leaderboard (rho drops to 0.51-0.54 for coding/math), while DualEval preserves rho 0.963-0.984, demonstrating static anchoring stabilizes rankings against noisy preference supervision. The Fisher-information subset-recovery experiment shows the top 10% most informative items recover full-data Spearman rho >= 0.95 across all three static-anchored domains. Synthetic contamination detection via clean-reference residuals achieves AUROC >= 0.995 (static) and AUROC 0.93-0.98 (arena) at injection rates of 0.5-5%. All experiments are at standard-benchmark scale (300-600 items, 18 models), not stress-scale (e.g., hundreds of models or tens of thousands of items). Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (a joint model-item latent space unifying static IRT and reward-distilled pairwise IRT) and NEW METHOD (Fisher-information-based item selection and residual-based anomaly detection operationalized on this joint fit). The individual components (2PL IRT, Bradley-Terry preference modeling, Fisher information for item selection) are each standard psychometric or ML tools; the novel contribution is combining static correctness and reward-model-derived preference signals into one shared latent scale with joint optimization, rather than fitting each source separately and merging post hoc as prior integration work (Li et al. 2024; Ni et al. 2024) does. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: a pair-treatment ablation (Table 5) isolates the contribution of both-bad anchoring and tie filtering, showing both-bad anchoring is the dominant factor (BB-AUC 0.903 vs 0.622 without it). A component-isolation ablation for Fisher scoring (Figure 2: top-Fisher vs sharpness-only vs difficulty-matched vs random) clarifies which factors drive informativeness. Held-out calibration is reported with ECE 0.04-0.07 (static) and 0.01-0.03 (arena) across three domains and three seeds. Bootstrap stability analysis (B=100, question-level cluster bootstrap stratified by source) reports per-model confidence intervals. However: no code, data, or model weights are released as of this preprint (no repository URL beyond a project webpage placeholder is confirmed functional in the text); the primary arena supervision relies on a single proprietary reward model, which is a reproducibility gap the authors acknowledge; each model-question pair is evaluated with a single generated response (no resampling), so item sharpness and residual anomalies may partly reflect response-level stochasticity rather than true model capability; arena prompts are filtered into domains using an LLM judge (gpt-5.4-mini) rather than human-verified domain labels, introducing a filtering dependency the paper does not stress-test. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper runs experiments across four domains with 18 real models and reports measured accuracy/AUROC/Spearman correlations). - Practical: Yes; a single domain's joint fit completes in 10-25 seconds on a CPU with single-precision floats, requiring no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Very low marginal cost beyond one static evaluation pass and one reward-model forward pass per response; the Adam optimization itself is lightweight (2000 epochs on a low-dimensional latent model). - Effective: The paper's core claims (static reconstruction, arena reconstruction, subset recovery, contamination detection) are measured directly via held-out accuracy, Spearman correlation, and AUROC/AUPRC, not proxies; however, the arena-side claims are only as strong as the reward model's alignment with human preference, which the paper itself measures at 0.715-0.875 pairwise accuracy against human judgments (Table 3), meaning up to ~13-29% of arena "ground truth" is itself noisy. - Scalable: Not tested; the paper evaluates 18 models with 300-600 items per domain and does not report runtime or accuracy at 10x-100x more models or items. Given the joint fit's O(M^2) pairwise arena comparisons per item (explicitly noted in the text), scaling to hundreds of models would increase arena-loss computation cost, which is not analyzed. Delivers: no software artifact, trained model, or dataset release is confirmed in the visible text (a project webpage and GitHub link are listed on the title page, but their contents are not described within the paper). The IRT/Fisher-information methodology is a reusable technique but the paper stops at describing it, without an explicit library or tool contribution. Strengths: - The both-bad anchoring mechanism for handling arena pairs where both models fail is a specific, well-ablated technical contribution (BB-AUC 0.903 vs 0.622), addressing a real failure mode of naive Bradley-Terry fitting on reward-derived pairs. - The Fisher-information item-selection diagnostic is a genuinely actionable output: it quantifies benchmark redundancy directly (top 10% of items recovering rho >= 0.95) rather than asserting redundancy qualitatively, with a concrete algorithmic recipe (Fisher information under the fitted IRT model) that other benchmark maintainers can apply. - Cross-RM robustness experiments (Table 2, Appendix F) provide a genuine stress test of the framework's central claim, showing static anchoring stabilizes rankings against a materially different reward model, not just the one the authors trained. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) primary arena supervision depends on their internal proprietary reward model, limiting direct reproducibility, though they partially address this with the public Skywork RM replication; (2) each model-question pair uses a single generated response, not capturing within-model sampling variability or multi-attempt performance; (3) the current formulation uses one scalar ability parameter per model per domain, which is interpretable but cannot capture multidimensional specialization across skills, task formats, or interaction settings. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The LLM-judge domain-filtering step (gpt-5.4-mini classifying arena prompts into coding/math/misc with confidence thresholds) is itself an unvalidated component; no human-agreement check is reported for this filter, yet it determines which arena prompts enter each domain's evaluation. - The paper's contamination-detection experiments use synthetic injection (flipping labels or adding a fixed reward offset) rather than real known-contamination cases; synthetic contamination may be easier to detect than organic contamination patterns (e.g., partial memorization, near-duplicate leakage), so the AUROC >= 0.995 figures may not transfer to real-world audit settings. - No comparison is made against existing IRT-based benchmark diagnostic tools cited in related work (e.g., Zhou et al. 2026, Castleman et al. 2025) on the same data, so DualEval's item-informativeness diagnostic is validated in isolation rather than benchmarked against the closest prior alternatives. - The reward-model validation against human preference (Table 3, N=120-376 pairs per domain) is a fairly small sample for establishing RM-human alignment claims that underpin the entire arena branch of the framework. Bottom Line: Practitioners maintaining LLM leaderboards or benchmark suites gain a concretely useful diagnostic (Fisher-information item selection and residual-based anomaly detection) from this work, but the central integration claim rests heavily on a single non-public reward model, so replication of the exact numbers reported here is not possible without access to that model or acceptance of the weaker public-RM results.
Recommended 2606.26429 PDF
#36 — Innovative (61)

EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments (39 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Jul 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) drive the score: Autonomous Policy Evolution is a genuine NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION, and the AST-topology synthesis-vs-tuning edit classifier cleanly separates two distinct failure modes (fails-to-discover vs. fails-to-refine) that a bare leaderboard conflates. The strict hidden-validation visibility boundary is a clean anti-contamination design, and code plus a benchmark artifact are released. Rigor (50) is the ceiling: single run per agent-environment cell with no seeds or error bars (the paper's own 'preliminary experiments'), and GPT-5.5's Codex harness is confounded against the other agents' Claude Code harness, so the specific 0.891-vs-0.750 rankings carry unquantified single-run noise. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*62 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR: - What it does: This paper introduces EvoPolicyGym, a benchmark that measures how well a coding agent iteratively rewrites an executable RL policy under a fixed 128-episode interaction budget, scoring the final policy on hidden validation and held-out returns while also recording the full edit trajectory. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks collapse iterative self-improvement into a single final score, hiding whether an agent found the right control mechanism early, churned uselessly, or got lucky late, which matters as coding agents are increasingly deployed to maintain and evolve live decision-making systems rather than produce one-shot patches. - How it does it: The benchmark enforces a strict visibility boundary (agents see only train-episode rollouts and feedback; validation/held-out selection is server-side and hidden) and classifies every code-editing transition as a synthesis edit (new AST topology) versus a parametric edit (same topology, tuned constants), letting the same data support both a leaderboard and a mechanism-level diagnostic. - The good: The synthesis/tuning AST-based edit classifier (Table 4, Figure 4) cleanly separates two failure modes, agents that never discover a viable control structure (MiniMax-M3, DeepSeek-V4-Pro score 0.03-0.19 on synthesis-dominant tasks) from agents that discover but fail to refine one, giving actionable diagnostic signal beyond a bare leaderboard number. - The missing: The evaluation covers exactly one run per agent-environment cell (no seeds, no error bars, no variance estimate), so a 0.938 vs 0.750 Core16 gap between GPT-5.5 and Claude Opus 4.7 could partly reflect single-run noise rather than a reliable capability difference. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating coding agents that maintain live systems over multiple revisions; the trajectory-diagnostic methodology (edit-type classification, budget-allocation curves) is more valuable than the leaderboard itself, which rests on a small, single-seed 16-environment suite. Problem & Motivation: Autonomous coding agents increasingly maintain and revise executable artifacts (reward functions, controllers, heuristics, ML pipelines) rather than emit a single patch, but the standard evaluation protocols in this space, repository-level benchmarks like SWE-bench and self-improvement studies like Reflexion or AlphaEvolve, either measure one-shot correctness or report only a final aggregate score. That final-score collapse hides trajectory-level failure modes documented in prior agent-behavior studies (blind retries, brittle overfitting to visible feedback, unverified fixes) and confounds genuine policy-search capability with open-ended software-engineering skill. The paper targets researchers and benchmark designers who need a controlled setting that isolates an agent's ability to convert bounded, server-mediated environment feedback into a policy that generalizes to hidden held-out cases, while still capturing the iterative decisions (what to submit, when to explore vs. exploit, how much budget to spend) that make autonomous improvement hard to evaluate. Key Idea: The central mechanism is a strict visibility boundary combined with an AST-based edit classifier. Agents only ever see train-episode rollouts and platform-written feedback; checkpoint selection (via hidden validation) and final scoring (via hidden held-out evaluation) happen entirely server-side and are never revealed during the run. Layered on top, every score-bearing submission is mechanically classified, by comparing the numeric-constant-stripped AST topology of the submitted policy source bundle against the prior submission, as either a synthesis edit (new topology: the agent built new task machinery) or a parametric edit (same topology: the agent tuned existing constants). This turns a black-box optimization trace into an interpretable structural narrative without requiring semantic code understanding. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Protocol layer: an agent (harness + LLM) is given a workspace containing a policy.py entry point (reset/act interface), an episode budget (128 for Core16), and a local HTTP service (/info, /task, /submit); each accepted submit snapshots the workspace, charges the requested episode count against budget, and returns structured feedback (episode returns, trajectory records, errors, diagnostics) without modifying the agent's workspace directly. (2) Scoring layer: after budget exhaustion, the server evaluates every valid checkpoint on a 16-case hidden validation split, selects the checkpoint with highest validation mean return (ties broken by recency), and reports that checkpoint's mean return on a 32-case hidden held-out split; cross-environment aggregation uses within-environment descending rank (s_{m,e} = 1 - (rank_e(m)-1)/(N_e-1)) rather than raw returns, since reward scales are not comparable across the 16 tasks. (3) Diagnostic layer: post-hoc, the paper computes best-so-far hidden-validation curves over consumed budget (Figure 3) and the synthesis/tuning edit classification (Table 4), plus a random-to-best normalization (norm_{m,e} = clip[0,1]((R^heldout - R^random)/(R^best - R^random))) to compare relative performance within synthesis-dominant versus tuning-dominant environment subsets. Three design decisions stand out: using rank-based rather than return-based aggregation is well-justified given genuinely incomparable reward scales across Acrobot, CarRacing, and FetchPush; using AST topology after constant-stripping as a proxy for 'new mechanism' is a reasonable but explicitly acknowledged proxy (two topologies can implement similar behavior); and running a single 128-episode budget with no seed replication is a scope-limiting choice the paper itself flags as producing 'preliminary experiments,' which weakens confidence in fine-grained agent-to-agent comparisons. Key Results: On the Core16 suite (16 environments across Gym/Box2D, MuJoCo, MiniGrid, and Robotics/Driving families, 128-episode budget, 16 hidden validation + 32 hidden held-out cases per environment), GPT-5.5 (via Codex harness) obtains the highest Core16 rank score (0.891) with 9 wins and top-two placement on all 16 environments; Claude Opus 4.7 (Claude Code harness) is second (0.750, 5 wins, 12 top-two, best MiniGrid family score of 0.938); MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro score 0.531 and 0.359 respectively, each winning only one environment (HalfCheetah and Roundabout) while scoring near or below the uniform-random-policy reference (0.109) on several MiniGrid tasks (0.000 on three of four DoorKey/KeyCorridor/ObstructedMaze cases for both). On the synthesis/tuning split (Figure 4), GPT-5.5 and Claude Opus 4.7 reach 0.98 and 1.00 normalized held-out performance on synthesis-dominant tasks versus 0.19 and 0.03 for MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro; the four agents cluster more tightly (0.67-0.99) on tuning-dominant tasks. The edit-success table (Table 4) shows GPT-5.5 and Claude Opus 4.7 convert synthesis edits into new validation bests 41% and 48% of the time versus 10% and 3% for MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro. No conventional RL baseline (e.g., PPO trained to convergence) is included; the paper explicitly excludes them because the 128-episode budget is far below the sample regime where standard RL training converges, so the comparison is agent-vs-agent only, not agent-vs-RL. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION (Autonomous Policy Evolution as a distinct evaluated setting, separating policy-search capability from open-ended software-engineering benchmarks) plus NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET (EvoPolicyGym/Core16) plus NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the synthesis-vs-tuning edit-type diagnostic and its correlation with held-out performance). The genuinely novel piece is the combination of a strict hidden-validation/held-out visibility boundary with a mechanical, semantics-free edit-type classifier applied to iterative RL policy code; the underlying components (Gymnasium environments, rank-based cross-task aggregation, coding-agent harnesses) are standard and adapted rather than invented. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports no seeds, no error bars, and no repeated runs per agent-environment cell; each leaderboard cell is a single 128-episode run, which the paper itself labels 'preliminary experiments.' This is the paper's largest rigor gap given that LLM-agent behavior is known to be high-variance run-to-run. Ablations are present in a diagnostic sense (synthesis vs. tuning split, AST feature comparison in Table 3, edit-size vs. improvement-probability plot in Figure 8) but these describe agent behavior rather than validating benchmark design choices (e.g., no ablation on hidden validation set size, budget size, or the effect of removing the visibility boundary). Code, environment adapters, and experiment data are released at stated GitHub/EvoPolicyGym-Exp-data/EvoPolicyGym.io links (URLs given but not verified here). No Goodhart's Law risk from self-referential benchmarking (validation/held-out splits are genuinely hidden from the agent), which is a methodological strength. Standard baselines (conventional RL) are explicitly and reasonably excluded rather than omitted silently. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (the paper runs four harness-model agents on 16 environments and reports measured held-out returns). - Practical: Yes; the benchmark server, Gymnasium environments, and coding-agent harnesses (Codex, Claude Code) all run on commodity hardware with API-based LLM calls, no specialized infrastructure required. - Efficient: Not deeply analyzed as a cost metric; token/cost accounting (Appendix C.2) is reported as diagnostic only and varies substantially by harness (e.g., Claude Opus 4.7 uses up to 44.19M cache tokens on CarRacing versus GPT-5.5's 11.36M), but these numbers are excluded from the leaderboard score, so the paper does not state a normalized efficiency comparison. - Effective: Partially; the leaderboard shows agents solve most Core16 tasks well above random, but three of four MiniGrid tasks are solved by only one or two of four agents (0.000 return for MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro), so 'effective' varies sharply by task family rather than holding uniformly. - Scalable: Not tested. The paper explicitly does not test larger budgets (128 episodes is called 'far below the sample regime' for RL convergence) or a larger environment suite beyond Core16; the adapter layer claims broader Gymnasium-family coverage (Atari, MiniWoB++, MetaWorld) but this is asserted as future task reservoir, not validated. The framework does deliver a software artifact: the benchmark harness, environment adapters, and released experiment data, rather than stopping at paper form. Strengths: - The visibility-boundary design (train feedback visible, validation/held-out server-side and never revealed) is a genuinely clean solution to a common contamination problem in agent self-improvement evaluation, preventing the agent from directly optimizing against its scoring signal. - The AST-topology-based synthesis/tuning edit classifier is a simple, mechanically reproducible diagnostic that requires no LLM judgment and cleanly separates two distinct failure modes (failure to discover structure vs. failure to refine structure) that a bare leaderboard number would conflate. - The qualitative feedback-utilization traces (Figure 7, CarRacing) and code-phase timelines (Figures 5-6) connect scalar score changes to actual causal edits (e.g., GPT-5.5 attributing lane-departure failures to 'weak late-bend recognition' and revising lookahead logic), giving interpretable evidence beyond aggregate statistics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state these diagnostics are 'conservative proxies, not semantic proofs': AST topology can miss behaviorally similar implementations under different topologies, and the policy-source-bundle boundary excludes learned weights and generated data files, understating some policy complexity. They also note the synthesis/tuning split is 'a lens rather than a taxonomy' since some tasks (Bipedal, CarRacing) benefit from both modes sequentially rather than being purely one or the other. They confront these limitations directly in a dedicated Section 5.3. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No repeated runs or seeds per agent-environment cell; the paper's own language ('preliminary experiments') signals awareness that this is a single-run leaderboard, but the analysis (Section 4.2) still draws fairly specific comparative conclusions (e.g., ranking agents by 0.891 vs. 0.750) without any variance estimate to support the precision implied. - The four evaluated agents are not harness-matched: GPT-5.5 runs on the Codex harness while the other three run on Claude Code, so harness effects and model effects are confounded throughout the leaderboard, a limitation the paper acknowledges only obliquely ('we include the harness as part of the evaluation dimension') rather than as a threat to the model-level conclusions readers are likely to draw. - The two trajectory case studies (CarRacing, BipedalWalker) are stated to be 'randomly sampled' but this claim is not independently verifiable from the paper, and with only two case studies out of 16 environments, the qualitative narrative in Section 5.2 covers a narrow slice of the full behavioral space. - Core16's 16 environments, while spanning four families, is a modest suite; the adapter layer claims broader coverage (Atari, WebArena-style, MetaWorld) as a 'task reservoir; but none of that broader coverage is experimentally validated in this paper, so external validity beyond Core16 is unestablished. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating coding agents for iterative, feedback-driven system maintenance gains a reusable diagnostic methodology (visibility boundary plus AST-based edit classification) from this paper, but should treat the specific agent rankings as a single-run, harness-confounded preliminary result rather than a settled capability comparison.
Recommended 2607.02440 PDF
#37 — Innovative (61)

Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models (22 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models (MBD-LMs), obtained by post-training Block Diffusion Language Models (BD-LMs) with Multi-block Teacher Forcing (MultiTF), plus a Block Buffer inference engine that makes concurrent multi-block decoding executable with static input shapes. - Why it's important: Block Diffusion Language Models serve efficiently via KV caching but decode one block at a time, leaving inter-block parallelism unused and creating KV-cache-storing bubbles that cap decoding throughput. - How it does it: MultiTF trains on bounded noise-groups of consecutive blocks with a randomized chain-uniform noise-scheduler and a Group-Aware Dual-Stream Mask, so the model sees noisy-block visibility patterns that match a fixed-size running-set at inference, and a Block Buffer decouples this logical running-set from a static physical input for CUDA Graph replay. - The good: MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax reaches an average Tokens Per Forward pass of 9.34 (+169% over native LLaDA2-Mini) with only a 1.02-point accuracy drop, and this converts into a measured 926.67 average TPS on two H100 GPUs versus 517.16 for the SingleBD baseline (+79.19%), directly validating the wall-clock claim rather than resting on TPF alone. - The missing: All accuracy results come from a single training/evaluation run per configuration; no seeds, variance, or significance tests are reported across the four benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, MBPP+, HumanEval+). - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or serving block diffusion LLMs (LLaDA2.x, SDAR family) who wants inter-block parallelism without retraining from scratch; skip if you work purely on fully bidirectional (non-block) diffusion LMs, since the running-set formulation is specific to block-causal architectures.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (68) is the strongest dimension: the running-set unification of teacher-forcing and D2F as endpoints of a single parameter, plus a coverage proof, is a coherent new formulation rather than a raw engineering fix. Rigor (58) is credible (component ablations plus a roofline latency model validated against measured wall-clock TPS on H100s, not just TPF proxies), but it is pulled down by single-run point estimates with no seeds or significance tests. Resilience (55) and Completeness (55) suffer from the narrow model-family scope (all LLaDA2.x/SDAR, no comparison against a fully-retrained competing MultiBD method) and the absence of a dedicated limitations section. Weighted composite is 60.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Block Diffusion Language Models (BD-LMs, e.g., LLaDA2.x, SDAR) address the KV-caching and variable-length limitations of fully bidirectional diffusion LMs by decoding block-by-block with block-causal attention. However, their native Single-Block Diffusion (SingleBD) inference must finish decoding and caching one block before the next can start, creating storing bubbles that leave inter-block parallelism unused. The Discrete Diffusion Forcing (D2F) training strategy from prior work exposes multi-block visibility during training but creates a train-inference mismatch: D2F trains on an unbounded noisy suffix with a monotonic noise schedule and full-attention prefix conditioning, whereas practical multi-block inference (MultiBD) operates over a small, bounded set of concurrently active blocks (a running-set, empirically size around 2-4) with heterogeneous per-slot noise levels, and is incompatible with the standard prefix-caching interface of BD-LMs. Key Idea: Reformulate the D2F-vs-Teacher-Forcing distinction as two endpoints of a single running-set-size parameter, then train directly on synthetic states that mimic the actual bounded, heterogeneously-noised running-set rather than an unbounded monotonically-noised suffix. The training-side lever is a chain-uniform noise-scheduler that samples each block's mask ratio as an increasing draw conditioned on the previous block's ratio within a small noise-group, which produces the same kind of large slot-to-slot noise gaps observed empirically during MultiBD decoding (Figure 2), rather than the D2F-style monotonic schedule that produces overlapping, poorly separated slot-wise noise distributions. Methodology: MultiTF post-training (Algorithm 1) proceeds per clean sequence: (1) partition into blocks and construct a set of group-layouts, both systematic (shifted layouts enumerating every noise-group size from 2 to a max G_max and every group-relative offset, giving formal coverage guarantees proven in Appendix A) and random (sampled group sizes for added distributional diversity); (2) for each noise-group, sample per-block mask ratios via the chain-uniform scheduler (a group-level floor, then each block draws uniformly between the running floor and an effective ceiling, updating the floor); (3) build an input sequence concatenating the noisy sequence with the clean sequence and apply a Group-Aware Dual-Stream Mask with three sub-blocks: group-diagonal (noisy blocks see same-group noisy blocks from same/preceding block index), group-offset-causal (noisy tokens condition on clean prefix before their group), and block-causal (standard clean-to-clean visibility); (4) optimize masked cross-entropy only on masked noisy positions (Eq. 3.4), optionally stacked with model-specific objectives such as DMax's. On the inference side, the Block Buffer mechanism (Section 3.3, Algorithm 5) maintains a fixed number of physical block slots per request; blocks transition DUMMY to ACTIVE to TO_CACHE to IN_CACHE, so the running-set can slide forward by activating existing dummy slots and committing front blocks to KV cache, keeping the physical input shape static for CUDA Graph capture/replay while preserving prefix-cache reuse. Three design decisions stand out: the chain-uniform scheduler is justified empirically via ablation (Table 2b) and via alignment analysis (Figure 2) matching training-noise to inference-time mask-ratio distributions; the systematic+random layout combination is justified by ablation showing complementary contributions (structured coverage vs. distributional diversity); the choice of running-set as the unifying abstraction is supported by a coverage proof (Appendix A, Eq. A.5-A.7) bounding the training-inference objective gap by running-set and noise-ratio mismatch terms, though this bound assumes bounded/Lipschitz loss properties (A2, A3) that are stated but not empirically verified. Key Results: On four benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, MBPP+, HumanEval+), MBD-LLaDA2-Mini raises average Tokens Per Forward pass (TPF) from 3.47 (native SingleBD) to 6.19 (+78.4%) while improving average accuracy from 79.95% to 81.03%. Combined with the T2T acceleration method DMax, MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax reaches average TPF of 9.34 (+169.16% vs. plain LLaDA2-Mini, +47.1% vs. LLaDA2-Mini-DMax) with only a 1.02-point average accuracy drop (78.57% vs. 78.17% baseline DMax, and vs. 79.59% native). Measured wall-clock throughput on two H100 GPUs (TP=2) confirms the TPF gains translate to practice: MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax achieves 926.67 average TPS vs. 517.16 for LLaDA2-Mini (+79.19%), with per-step latency increasing only 1.58x (Table 3). Training-free MultiBD (no MultiTF) already raises TPF but drops accuracy substantially on some models (e.g., LLaDA2-Mini: 78.59% training-free vs. 81.03% with MultiTF), demonstrating the value of the alignment step. Generalization is shown across the SDAR family (SDAR-8B-Chat-b32: TPF 2.54 to 4.46, +75.6%, accuracy 69.00% to 69.74%) and to untested model variants (LLaDA2-Mini-CAP, LLaDA2.1-Mini) without any retraining. Baselines (SingleBD-native, training-free MultiBD) are the paper's own prior-generation configurations rather than external state-of-the-art competitor systems; there is no comparison against other MultiBD-style training methods (e.g., d3LLM, LightningRL) on the same benchmarks, only citation in Related Work. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (unifying TF and D2F as endpoints of a running-set-size parameter) plus NEW METHOD (MultiTF training procedure: bounded noise-groups, chain-uniform scheduler, Group-Aware Dual-Stream Mask) plus NEW SYSTEM (Block Buffer inference engine for static-shape MultiBD execution). The genuinely novel piece is the explicit training-inference alignment argument and its systematic-layout coverage proof; the underlying building blocks (masked diffusion training, block-causal attention, CUDA-Graph-friendly static shapes, KV caching) are standard and combined rather than invented. Rigor & Reproducibility: Table 2b provides component-level ablations (noise-group layout construction, noise-scheduler choice) isolating which design decisions matter, and these ablations show clear, non-trivial accuracy/TPF trade-offs (e.g., chain-uniform vs. D2F-style scheduler: 84.59% vs. 79.34% accuracy at similar TPF). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper; all reported accuracy and TPF numbers appear to be single-run point estimates. No code, model weights, or data release is mentioned in the main text; a project page URL is given (sjtu-deng-lab.github.io/mbd-lms) but its contents are not described. Evaluation uses standard external math (GSM8K, MATH500) and code (MBPP+, HumanEval+) benchmarks rather than an author-constructed dataset, mitigating one common failure mode, but all models tested are from the authors' own lab or closely affiliated model families (LLaDA2.x, SDAR), so there is no independent replication of the training method on an unrelated BD-LM architecture. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (with a SYSTEMS component for the inference engine). Practical: Yes, evaluated on standard H100 GPUs with tensor parallelism degree 2, a realistic production configuration. Efficient: MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax needs 1.58x higher per-step latency (7.07ms to 11.20ms) to deliver 2.69x higher TPF, and the Block Buffer approach explicitly trades some token efficiency (processing dummy slots) for parallelism, quantified via the roofline-style FLOPs/memory-traffic model in Section 4.5. Effective: The paper measures both TPF/AUP (proxy for the accuracy-parallelism trade-off) and end-to-end TPS on real hardware, which is a stronger effectiveness case than TPF alone since it demonstrates the gains are not consumed by increased per-step cost. Scalable: Not tested beyond the reported buffer sizes (2 or 4) and running-set expectation of about 2, with occasional expansion to 3-4 (Figure 2D); the paper does not test what happens at substantially larger block-buffer sizes or running-set sizes, nor at longer generation lengths or larger models than the 8B SDAR/LLaDA2-Mini variants evaluated. The FLOPs model (Section 4.5) predicts diminishing returns as buffer size grows via increased attention and dummy-slot overhead, but this is analytical, not empirically validated at scale. Strengths: - The running-set unification of TF and D2F as parametrized extremes gives a coherent explanation for why D2F's native multi-block inference underperforms, backed by a formal coverage argument (Appendix A) rather than just an empirical fix. - The efficiency analysis (Section 4.5, Table 3) goes beyond the common practice of reporting only algorithmic parallelism metrics (TPF) by deriving a roofline-style latency model and validating predicted throughput scaling (1.44x predicted vs. 1.44x measured for LLaDA2-Mini) against measured wall-clock TPS on real GPUs. - The ablation in Table 2b isolates two independent design axes (layout construction, noise scheduler) and shows they are complementary, not redundant, which is a stronger form of evidence than a single combined ablation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section or explicitly discuss failure modes, scope restrictions, or scenarios where MultiTF might underperform. The Appendix's theoretical bound (Eq. A.5) implicitly acknowledges an approximation gap between the ideal MultiBD objective and the MultiTF surrogate, controlled by running-set and noise-ratio mismatch terms, which is the closest the paper comes to naming a limitation, but this is framed as a proof of near-optimality rather than a caveat. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No seeds/variance reporting across any of the four benchmarks, so the size of headline gains (e.g., 1.02-point accuracy drop) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run noise. - The theoretical bound in Appendix A relies on assumptions (bounded loss, Lipschitz noise-ratio dependence, bounded Wasserstein-1 mismatch) that are stated but never estimated or verified empirically for the actual trained models, so the practical tightness of the bound is unknown. - All evaluated base models come from labs closely affiliated with the authors (LLaDA2.x, SDAR), and no comparison is run against competing multi-block training methods (D2F itself is only compared as a training-free heuristic and as a scheduler ablation, not as a fully retrained alternative baseline using its own paradigm). - Evaluation is limited to math and code benchmarks; no results on open-ended generation, long-context tasks, or instruction-following quality where degenerate token choices under high parallelism might show up differently than in exact-match math/code metrics. Bottom Line: Engineers already running block diffusion LLMs (LLaDA2.x, SDAR) in production gain a concrete, empirically validated recipe for roughly doubling decoding throughput with under 1.5 points of accuracy loss, verified against wall-clock GPU measurements rather than parallelism proxies alone, though the single-run reporting and narrow model-family scope warrant a cautious read of the exact numeric gains.
Recommended 2606.29215 PDF
#38 — Innovative (60)

CrossPool: Efficient Multi-LLM Serving for Cold MoE Models through KV-Cache and Weight Disaggregation

Z Ye, T Wo, D Xue, M Zhang, Y Teng, C Hu, R Yang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CrossPool, a serving engine that disaggregates FFN weights and KV-cache into two separate GPU memory pools for colocating many cold Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, keeping attention local to KV-cache and exchanging only hidden states across the pool boundary. - Why it's important: Datacenters increasingly host many rarely-used MoE models whose static weight footprint crowds out the shared GPU memory needed for KV-cache, capping long-context capacity and wasting hardware under skewed, low-concurrency traffic (the paper cites ~90% of models on OpenRouter consuming only 16.5% of tokens). - How it does it: It splits the monolithic GPU memory pool so FFN weights sit in a dedicated weights pool and attention/KV-cache sit in a dedicated KV-cache pool, connected via NVSHMEM over NVLink, with a layer-wise pipeline scheduler and persistent GPU-resident kernels to hide the added cross-pool communication and control overhead. - The good: On a 5-A100 testbed serving three real 10-30B MoE models, CrossPool sustains positive throughput across LongAlign context-length bins where Static Partition and kvcached hit hard capacity cliffs, while cutting P99 time-between-tokens (TBT) by up to 10.4x over kvcached at 0.8 RPS. - The missing: All results come from a single 5-GPU, 3-model configuration; there is no experiment varying GPU count, model count, or model heterogeneity, so the claimed scalability of the disaggregation idea is not measured beyond this one setup. - Read it or not: Worth reading for systems researchers and engineers building multi-tenant LLM serving infrastructure who face cold-model GPU memory pressure; the core weights/KV-cache disaggregation idea and its engineering solutions (layer-wise pipelining, persistent kernels) are directly reusable, though the evaluation is narrow enough that production claims need independent validation at larger scale.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (60) and Novelty (62): the weight/KV-cache disaggregation into separate pools is not new in isolation (MegaScale-Infer, Step-3), but applying it to the cold-model low-concurrency KV-stranding problem with a trace-driven planner and persistent-kernel control-lowering is a genuine contribution, delivered as a modified SGLang runtime (no release). Rigor (55) has a clean complementary-mechanism ablation but single 3-model/5-GPU config with no sweep over model or GPU count, no seeds, and 'estimated' context-length scalability. Completeness (62) and Resilience (58) are limited by the abstract's up-to-10.4x framing being relative only to kvcached, not the latency-optimal Static Partition. Weighted: 0.15*62+0.15*55+0.10*60+0.15*62+0.15*58+0.30*60 = 60.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM serving platforms (DeepInfra, Alibaba Model Studio, Volcano Engine) increasingly host many MoE models to meet agentic-application demand, but request popularity is highly skewed: most models are "cold" and receive sparse traffic, yet each cold model's weights must remain resident in GPU memory, consuming a large fixed footprint. Simultaneously, modern MoE models increasingly use KV-head-limited attention (MQA, MLA, GQA with few heads) for efficiency, but serving engines place these under data parallelism (DP) to boost aggregate throughput; under cold, low-concurrency traffic, only the DP replica holding an active request can expose its KV-cache to that request, so the reachable long-context capacity per request shrinks even though aggregate GPU memory is plentiful. Existing multi-LLM serving systems (MuxServe, kvcached/Chimera) improve GPU sharing but still colocate static weights and dynamic KV-cache in one monolithic pool, so weight footprint directly reduces per-GPU KV-cache capacity available to any given model. Key Idea: Model weights and KV-cache have fundamentally different lifetimes: weights are model-determined and stable, KV-cache is demand-determined and transient. By physically separating them into two GPU memory pools and moving only hidden states (bounded by batch size x hidden dimension) across the pool boundary rather than KV-cache tensors (which grow with context length), the system can consolidate FFN weights from many cold models into a shared weights pool while giving each active request access to a much larger, dynamically shared KV-cache pool than a monolithic per-model partition would allow. Methodology: The pipeline has three components addressing three stated design challenges (C1-C3). (1) KV-cache planner and virtualizer (addresses C1, heterogeneous KV-cache demand): an offline planner uses per-model prompt/output/service-time samples, arrival rate, page size, and per-token KV bytes to compute a P95/P99 trace-driven Monte Carlo quantile (Eq. 1-2) of aggregate active KV-cache demand across models, then sets a shared pool page budget; an online virtualizer exposes this via CUDA VMM-based paged addressing so attention kernels see a normal logical KV-cache interface while physical pages are mapped on demand. (2) Layer-wise pipeline scheduler (addresses C2, cross-pool communication): maintains two in-flight batches (potentially from different models with different layer counts) and interleaves attention execution in the KV-cache pool with FFN execution in the weights pool, overlapping hidden-state transfer with compute; supports early-exit for batches without a global layer barrier. (3) Persistent kernels with control lowering (addresses C3, CUDA-graph/mixed-scheduling overhead): captures attention and FFN subgraphs separately during warmup and dispatches them from GPU-resident polling kernels rather than host-driven per-layer launches, keeping the high-frequency scheduling/communication loop off the CPU-GPU control path. Implementation is built atop SGLang v0.5.10, replacing FFN layers in the KV-cache-pool runtime with proxy layers that ship hidden states to the weights pool via an NVSHMEM transport and receive FFN outputs back; current graph capture is limited to batch sizes 1-4. Three critical design decisions: (a) partitioning by FFN-vs-non-FFN rather than by layer or by request is justified by Table 1 showing FFN holds 94.5-95% of MoE parameters in the tested models; (b) using hidden-state exchange instead of KV-cache exchange is justified because hidden-state size doesn't grow with context length, though the paper concedes the transfer still sits on the critical path every layer/token; (c) provisioning by an aggregate P95/P99 Monte Carlo quantile rather than worst-case per-model sizing is justified as preserving empirical cross-dimension correlations, but the choice of quantile is a tunable design knob with no sensitivity analysis presented. Key Results: Evaluated on a 5x A100 (40GB HBM, NVLink) testbed with three real MoE models colocated (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GLM-4.7-Flash, DeepSeek-V2-Lite; ~154GB combined weights) against Static Partition (SGLang native fixed placement) and kvcached/Chimera (elastic KV memory management without weight/KV disaggregation). On ShareGPT balanced workloads at 0.8 RPS, CrossPool reduces P99 TBT versus kvcached by 7.6x (Qwen3-30B-A3B), 10.4x (DeepSeek-V2-Lite), and 7.3x (GLM-4.7-Flash); at 1.0 RPS the reductions are 2.1x, 5.0x, and 2.2x. On LongAlign long-context workloads, Static Partition and kvcached hit hard capacity cliffs (vertical drops in max-RPS-vs-context-length plots) as context grows, while CrossPool sustains positive throughput across the tested range. An ablation on ShareGPT at 0.5 RPS/model shows persistent kernels alone give 1.41x aggregate decode throughput (55.42 to 77.86 tok/s), the layer-wise scheduler alone gives 1.10x (60.83 tok/s), and combined they give 2.01x (111.40 tok/s). Static Partition has the lowest absolute latency throughout (fixed isolated placement per model) but cannot support the long-context pooling CrossPool provides. All experiments use exactly 3 models on 5 GPUs; no results at larger model counts, different GPU counts, or larger models are reported. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL combined with ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The core disaggregation idea (splitting attention/KV-cache from FFN across pools) is not new in isolation, as the paper itself notes MegaScale-Infer and Step-3 already disaggregate attention and FFN computation for MoE inference; CrossPool's genuine contribution is applying this disaggregation specifically to solve the cold-model, low-concurrency KV-cache-sharing problem (rather than throughput scaling for hot serving) and combining it with a trace-driven KV-cache planner, virtualized paging across cold models, and the specific layer-wise pipeline scheduler plus persistent-kernel control-lowering mechanisms to make the resulting cross-pool communication overhead tractable. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation study (Table 3) cleanly isolates the contribution of the two overhead-reduction mechanisms and shows they compose (2.01x combined vs. 1.41x and 1.10x individually), which is a genuine strength. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, multiple seeds, or repeated-trial variance are reported anywhere in the TBT or throughput figures; all numbers appear to be single-run point estimates. No code, weights, or artifact release URL is mentioned anywhere in the paper. Evaluation uses exactly 3 models on exactly 5 GPUs; there is no sweep over model count, GPU count, or heterogeneity, so it is unclear whether reported gains generalize beyond this specific configuration. Baselines (Static Partition, kvcached/Chimera) are current (Chimera is cited as a 2026 USENIX OSDI paper) and represent genuine alternative approaches, not strawmen. The context-length scalability results (Fig. 6) are described as "estimated" from sampled LongAlign context-length bins rather than measured end-to-end serving runs to failure, which is a weaker form of evidence than the directly-measured TBT results. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built serving engine evaluated by deployment on production-grade hardware). Practical: Yes, runs on commercially available A100 GPUs with standard NVLink interconnect and is built on SGLang v0.5.10, a widely used open-source serving engine, so integration cost for SGLang users is plausible though not demonstrated for other backends (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM). Efficient: The paper reports throughput and TBT gains but does not report the memory or engineering overhead of maintaining two separate CUDA-graph-captured pools, nor the cost of persistent-kernel GPU occupancy versus compute kernels; the ablation shows the mechanisms recover most of the overhead they introduce, but the paper does not quantify the overhead's absolute worst case (e.g., a burst that saturates both pools simultaneously). Effective: Yes, the LongAlign scalability results and ShareGPT TBT results directly measure the stated problem (long-context serving under GPU memory pressure with cold models) rather than a loose proxy. Scalable: Not tested. The evaluation is fixed at 3 models and 5 GPUs; the paper's own Discussion section (Limitations) acknowledges that heterogeneous model compute times create pipeline imbalance and that communication overhead is only partially hidden, but does not test at 10x-100x more models or GPUs, so scalability claims rest on architectural argument rather than measurement. Delivers a modified SGLang runtime (software artifact) though the code is not stated as released.
