Scalable: the author is explicit that findings come from two northern-European, EU-regulated energy contexts (Denmark and Germany) and cannot be assumed to transfer to other regulatory environments, sub-sectors (nuclear, gas distribution), or non-EU jurisdictions; no claim of scalability beyond this is made, which is appropriately cautious. The work delivers no software artifact; it stops at a conceptual governance-readiness model and a set of practice-oriented organisational/governance recommendations, explicitly labelled preliminary and requiring broader empirical validation before being treated as confirmed guidance. Strengths: - Cross-source convergence used correctly as an analytical signal: several key findings (domain-specificity failure, unresolved accountability, skill degradation) are explicitly flagged only when independently corroborated by participants from different organisations, countries, and professional roles, rather than over-claiming from single-source evidence, and the thesis is careful to label single-source findings as such throughout. - Honest treatment of evidentiary asymmetry: the analysis explicitly identifies that operators are drawing high-stakes operational conclusions from low-stakes administrative AI experience (the "asymmetry" in Finding 5.1.1), a genuinely useful and non-obvious observation about how trust judgments generalise incorrectly across contexts. - The pre-deployment trust-calibration-formation-stage concept is a real conceptual contribution: existing trust-calibration literature (Lee and See; Wischnewski et al.) treats calibration as something that develops through operational exposure to a deployed system, and this thesis identifies a prior formation stage the literature has not examined, with a plausible practical consequence (that "fixing" the eventual operational AI system may not correct trust judgments already locked in from unrelated administrative-tool experience). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The thesis states six limitations directly and without minimizing them: the three-interview empirical base prevents statistical generalisation; geographic/sectoral scope is limited to Denmark/Germany under EU regulatory frameworks; all three participants are pre-operational-deployment, meaning the study's most original claim (pre-deployment trust formation) cannot be checked against what actually happens post-deployment; sample heterogeneity (one participant is an academic researcher, not a practitioner); researcher positionality/confirmation bias risk; and unvalidated translation of the German-language interview. This is an unusually thorough and specific limitations section for a master's thesis, and the author frames these as "analytical parameters" for reading the findings rather than disclaimers, which is the correct posture. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The research question asks how organisational and governance structures "shape the responsible adoption and effective operational integration" of GenAI systems, but because none of the three organisations has deployed such a system operationally, the study cannot actually observe shaping of adoption or integration; it can only observe anticipated barriers and pre-deployment attitudes. The thesis's own Chapter 7.1 opening ("the short answer... is that they do not yet, because they are incomplete") effectively concedes the research question is not yet answerable with the available evidence, though this concession is somewhat buried relative to how prominently the four "contributions" are framed. - The four "contributions to existing literature" (skill degradation as governance risk, pre-deployment trust formation, absorptive capacity as prerequisite, integration failure as governance problem) are asserted with more confidence in the Discussion and Conclusion framing than the three-interview base can support; the limitations section says this explicitly, but the contribution language elsewhere ("this thesis establishes," "this study contributes") reads as stronger than one exploratory qualitative study with three participants should claim, an internal tension between the modest epistemic status disclosed in Chapter 4/7.3 and the more assertive framing in the abstract and Chapter 7.2/8. - No triangulation via document analysis (e.g., actual TSO AI governance policies, procedures, or the sprint-experiment plans referenced) was conducted despite the methodology chapter noting it as a possible supplementary method "where accessible"; it is unclear whether this was attempted and failed, or not pursued, and its absence means every governance claim rests solely on what participants say about their organisation's practices rather than on independent verification. Bottom Line: A practitioner scoping AI governance strategy for a safety-critical operator gains a structured pre-deployment checklist and a genuinely original observation about early trust-formation risk, but a researcher looking for evidence on how trust calibration or accountability actually function once generative AI is deployed in a control room will need to look elsewhere, since this study's design cannot speak to that by its own admission.
Search Results
#67 — Moderately Innovative (48)

LiveEdit: Towards Real-Time Diffusion-Based Streaming Video Editing (70 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LiveEdit, a streaming video editing framework built on a three-stage distillation pipeline (bidirectional foundation tuning, causal teacher-forcing, and 4-step DMD) plus an AR-oriented Mask Cache that reuses self-attention features in unedited background regions. - Why it's important: Real-time, chunk-by-chunk video editing for AR and live-streaming applications requires both strict background preservation and sub-100ms per-frame latency, and existing bidirectional or generation-oriented streaming editors satisfy at most one of these constraints. - How it does it: A bidirectional DiT teacher is progressively distilled into a causal, 4-step generator via teacher forcing and Distribution Matching Distillation, while an L2-distance-derived binary editing mask lets the model skip full self-attention computation on unedited tokens and reuse cached features instead. - The good: The method beats all six baselines on Text Alignment (0.270 vs. InsV2V's 0.259) and Background Consistency (0.956) while cutting latency from 603.51s (InsV2V) to 6.41s and reaching 12.66 FPS, a 97.38x speedup over the strongest quality baseline (Lucy Edit) reported in Fig. 1. - The missing: The 120-pair benchmark, its construction protocol, and the automated metrics (TA/BC/MS/DD/AQ/IQ) are entirely self-collected and self-computed with no external validation, no released benchmark or code link, and no test at longer sequence lengths or higher resolutions than the 81-frame/3-frame-chunk setup used throughout. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on autoregressive video diffusion distillation or attention-caching mechanisms; the mask-cache ablation (Table 3, Fig. 7-8) is a clean, reusable design lesson even if you discount the headline SOTA claims.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (42) is the primary drag: the entire 120-pair benchmark, its construction protocol, and the six automated metrics are self-collected and self-computed with no external validation, no released code or benchmark, and no seeds (a 20-person user study adds some independent signal). Completeness (45) and Resilience (45) are also weak because there is no explicit limitations section and no test of camera motion, large edited regions, longer sequences, or higher resolution, all of which would stress the mask-cache redundancy assumption. Novelty (55) credits the genuinely useful pairing of teacher-forced causal distillation with self-attention-only caching, validated by the cosine-similarity diagnostic. Weighted composite is 48.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 42 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 45 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video editing systems built on bidirectional diffusion transformers require the entire video sequence before producing the first output frame, since they attend to both past and future tokens. This makes them unusable for AR, live-streaming, or other interactive applications that need frame-by-frame output as video streams in. Streaming video generation methods (StreamDiffusion, StreamDiffusionV2, Self-Forcing) solve the latency problem but are built for open-ended synthesis, not editing: they lack the region-specific control and strict pixel-level background preservation that editing demands. The gap is a streaming-capable editor that preserves unedited regions with the fidelity of an offline bidirectional model while running at interactive frame rates. Key Idea: Two combined insights. First, naively truncating a bidirectional model's attention to only past frames causes the attention distribution to flatten and spread uniformly across history (Fig. 3), destroying the localized attention pattern the model needs for accurate editing; the fix is to explicitly teacher-force a causal student toward the bidirectional teacher's attention distribution rather than just masking future tokens at inference time. Second, in editing (unlike open-ended generation), most spatial tokens in a chunk correspond to the unedited background and their self-attention features are highly redundant across denoising steps and chunks (cosine similarity ~0.893 in Fig. 8), so those tokens can bypass full computation and reuse cached features, while cross-attention and the FFN cannot (caching FFN degrades quality sharply per Table 3). Methodology: Stage 1 (Foundation Tuning) trains a bidirectional DiT (built on Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B) with full spatio-temporal attention on 20K video-video pairs from Ditto-1M, using channel-wise concatenation of the noisy latent and source latent (avoiding the quadratic cost of token-wise concatenation), supervised by standard noise-matching MSE (9K steps, lr 1e-5, batch 8, CFG-enabled). Stage 2 (Teacher Forcing) converts the model to a causal DiT with a chunk-wise causal attention mask M_causal restricting tokens to attend only to current and prior 3-frame chunks, fine-tuned for 20K additional steps with the same MSE loss but under the causal constraint, aligning the causal attention distribution to the bidirectional prior rather than letting it degrade to uniform attention. Stage 3 (DMD) initializes a 4-step generator G_θ directly from the Stage-2 causal weights (explicitly skipping the ODE-initialization phase that methods like Self-Forcing require), and trains it with both an MSE term and a Distribution Matching Distillation gradient computed from a frozen real-score model and a trainable fake-score model, both initialized from Stage-1 weights, over 10K steps at fixed timesteps [0,250,500,750]. At inference, the AR-oriented Mask Cache computes, per incoming chunk k, an L2 distance between the previous chunk's source and edited latents, thresholds it (dynamically set to prune 70% of tokens) to produce a binary spatial mask, and routes only mask=1 (actively edited) tokens through full self-attention/cross-attention/FFN computation while mask=0 tokens retrieve features from a token cache populated by the previous chunk. Three design decisions stand out: channel-wise (not token-wise) concatenation is justified by avoiding quadratic blowup, and is empirically motivated but not directly ablated against token-wise concatenation in this paper; teacher forcing is justified via the attention-distribution visualization in Fig. 3, a reasonably direct diagnostic; the choice to cache only self-attention (not FFN or cross-attention) is directly validated by the cosine-similarity histogram (Fig. 8) and the ablation in Table 3, the strongest empirical justification in the paper. Key Results: On a self-collected 120-pair benchmark, LiveEdit achieves TA 0.270, BC 0.956, MS 0.992, AQ 0.581, IQ 0.708 versus bidirectional baselines InsV2V (TA 0.259, BC 0.943), LucyEdit (TA 0.253, BC 0.943), VideoCoF (TA 0.245, BC 0.953) and streaming baselines StreamDiffusion, StreamDiffusionV2, StreamV2V (all BC ≤ 0.951, TA ≤ 0.259). Latency and FPS (Fig. 1): LiveEdit reaches 12.66 FPS / 6.41s for 81 frames versus InsV2V's 0.13 FPS / 603.51s (a 97.38x speedup on latency) and Lucy Edit's 1.05 FPS / 77.23s (12.05x on FPS). Ablation (Table 2) shows Stage 1 alone needs 100 NFEs with CFG at 197.48s latency; Stage 2 (still 100 NFEs) drops to 200.36s but enables streaming; Stage 3 compresses to 4 NFEs, no CFG, 7.89s latency, TA improving to 0.265 and IQ to 0.720. The mask-cache ablation (Table 3) shows caching self-attention improves TA to 0.270 and MS to 0.992 versus no-cache (0.265/0.991), while caching FFN instead collapses quality (TA 0.236, AQ 0.440, IQ 0.513). A 20-volunteer user study (supplementary) reports LiveEdit at 100% top-3 preference for Instruction Consistency, 87.5% top-3 for Background Preservation, and 95.8% top-3 for Overall Quality, versus the next-best baseline (LucyEdit) at 87.5%/87.5%/87.5% top-3 but only 12.5% definitive "Best" votes for Overall Quality. Baselines are current (2025-2026 vintage: LucyEdit, VideoCoF, InsV2V, StreamDiffusionV2, all published within the last year) and represent both major paradigms (bidirectional offline and streaming), a reasonably strong comparison set. All evaluation is on the authors' own 120-pair benchmark with no external dataset validation. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (three-stage distillation pipeline transitioning a bidirectional editing DiT to a 4-step causal streaming editor) and NEW METHOD/ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (AR-oriented Mask Cache selectively applied to self-attention only). NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (a dedicated 120-pair streaming-editing benchmark) is claimed but not released or described with construction details. The genuinely novel contribution is the combination of teacher-forced causal distillation with a cache mechanism scoped specifically to self-attention layers for editing (validated by the cosine-similarity analysis in Fig. 8); the DMD step-distillation stage and channel-wise conditioning are applications of existing techniques (DMD, Wan2.1 backbone) to a new setting rather than new formulations themselves. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: Table 2 isolates each of the three distillation stages, and Table 3 isolates the mask-cache placement (self-attention vs. FFN), both showing which components matter. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported for any table; single-run point estimates are given throughout. The 120-pair benchmark and its construction protocol are not described beyond "collected," and no external benchmark (e.g., a published video-editing benchmark) is used, so all quantitative comparisons are on the authors' own evaluation set, a Goodhart's Law risk if the benchmark was constructed with awareness of the method's strengths. No code, data, or model weights release is mentioned; the project page (live-edit.github.io) hosts qualitative video demos but the paper does not state code will be released. The user study (20 volunteers, supplementary) adds independent human evaluation, a positive for rigor, but its instructions and video sampling are not detailed in the visible sections. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a trained system evaluated by experiments and a human study). Practical: Trained on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs; inference hardware is not stated but the 1.3B-parameter backbone (Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B) and 79ms per-frame latency suggest single consumer or prosumer GPU deployment is plausible, though not explicitly tested. Efficient: 4 NFEs versus the 100 NFEs of the bidirectional foundation stage, plus 70% token pruning via the mask cache, yields 6.41s for an 81-frame clip versus 603.51s for InsV2V, a substantial and directly measured cost reduction. Effective: The evidence (six automated metrics plus a 20-person user study) supports the background-preservation and instruction-following claims directly, though all measurement is self-collected. Scalable: Not tested. All results are reported at 81 frames with 3-frame chunks; there is no experiment at longer sequences, higher resolution, or with more complex multi-object edits, so behavior at 10x-100x the tested scale is unknown and unclaimed by the authors themselves. Strengths: - The cosine-similarity diagnostic (Fig. 8) directly measuring self-attention versus FFN temporal redundancy is a genuine piece of evidence, not just an engineering assumption, and it correctly predicts the ablation outcome in Table 3. - The teacher-forcing stage is motivated by a concrete visualization of the attention-distribution shift (Fig. 3) rather than an unexamined architectural choice. - The staged initialization strategy (each stage initializes from the previous stage's weights, with Stage 3 explicitly skipping ODE initialization) is a practical engineering decision with a clear ablation (Table 2) showing progressive latency reduction without quality collapse. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state their method is "architected for general, high-fidelity streaming video editing across diverse scenarios" in contrast to EgoEdit's narrower egocentric scope (Supplementary A), implicitly conceding that generality claims rest on comparison to a single narrow prior work rather than broad domain testing. No explicit limitations section addresses failure modes, resolution/scale boundaries, or cases where the mask cache might fail (e.g., large or fast-moving edited regions, camera motion). The paper does not discuss what happens when the editing mask covers most of the frame (reducing the cache's benefit) or under significant camera movement where the background itself is non-static. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 70% token-pruning threshold for the mask cache is stated as "dynamically determined" but the mechanism for setting it per-chunk is not fully specified, and no sensitivity analysis is given for how quality degrades as the threshold varies. - No experiments test camera motion, multi-object edits, or edits that cover a majority of the frame (which would defeat the redundancy assumption the cache exploits); the demo cases (Figs. 1, 2, 3, 4) are dominated by single-object, mostly static-background clips. - The benchmark, being self-collected with no described construction methodology, cannot be independently verified for representativeness, and no external editing benchmark (if one exists in this nascent area) is used for cross-validation. - The paper does not report inference resolution or GPU used at test time, making the 12.66 FPS / 79ms-per-frame claims hard to contextualize against baseline hardware assumptions. Bottom Line: A practitioner building streaming video-editing systems gains a concrete, empirically grounded recipe (teacher-forced causal distillation plus self-attention-only caching) but should treat the SOTA claims cautiously given the self-collected benchmark and absence of released code, data, or error bars.
Recommended 2606.26740 PDF
#68 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Agent Behavior Mining : Generative AI Agent Governance in Business Processes

H Vu, M Körner, A Rebmann, G Kevorkian… · arxiv.org · business process mining or process mining - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Agent Behavior Mining (ABM), an event data model that extends XES with agent-specific attributes (reasoning traces, tool calls, token costs) so that generative AI agent execution can be analyzed with standard process mining tools (discovery, conformance checking, variant analysis). - Why it's important: Organizations deploying LLM-based agents in business processes cannot currently see why an agent made a decision or how much it cost, leaving compliance drift and runaway token spend undetected until after the fact. - How it does it: The model factors agent operations (call_llm, execute_tool, transfer_to_agent, agent_start/finish) into an AgentEvent hierarchy under the existing XES concept/org/time attributes, adding ai:* fields (response_thought, tool_arguments, tokens, error) aligned with OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions, so logs stay compatible with off-the-shelf process mining tools without extending the XES meta-model. - The good: The 371-case Order-to-Cash trace log concretely demonstrates conformance checking catching real non-deterministic violations (Order Agent omitting calculate_total; Barista Agent skipping estimate_prep_time before an insertion), showing the model captures failures that would be invisible in ordinary agent framework logs. - The missing: The practitioner study measures only perceived usefulness from 18 people exploring dashboards under lab conditions with induced failures on a single gpt-4.1-based scenario; no objective decision-accuracy outcomes, no test against a second LLM family, and no production deployment. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for BPM or AgentOps practitioners evaluating governance tooling for multi-agent deployments; the event schema is a reasonable, low-friction reference design, but the evaluation is exploratory and does not establish that ABM changes governance outcomes.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (35) and Rigor (42) are the weak dimensions: treating agent primitives as XES-compatible event types is a sensible but largely obvious interoperability move with no new algorithm, and the evaluation is an 18-person perceived-usefulness survey under lab conditions on a single gpt-4.1 scenario with no control condition or comparison against raw AgentOps traces. The public repo, requirements-traceability, and honest limitations help, but the work measures perception, not governance impact. Composite: 0.15*45 + 0.15*42 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*52 ≈ 46.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 42 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 52
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Business Process Management (BPM) governance depends on standardized, auditable, deterministic execution, but generative AI (GenAI) agents built on LLMs make probabilistic, opaque decisions. Process owners deploying multi-agent systems (MAS) for tasks like order-to-cash (O2C) handling cannot verify whether an agent applied an existing policy, hallucinated a new rule, or granted an undeserved refund, because agent frameworks expose outputs but not the reasoning or cost structure behind them. Existing AgentOps tools (LangSmith, LangFuse, Phoenix) provide token/latency traces for engineers debugging models, but produce unstructured traces that lack the standardized activity types needed for process discovery, conformance checking, or variant analysis. Prior event data standards (XES, Object-Centric Event Data, Agent System Event Data) either predate GenAI agents or focus on business objects/roles rather than reasoning traces and token costs, leaving a gap between agent observability tooling and BPM governance tooling. Key Idea: There is no single novel algorithmic insight; the core move is a schema design decision: treat GenAI-specific execution primitives (LLM calls, tool executions, agent handoffs, reasoning traces, token costs) as first-class XES-compatible event types and attributes, rather than inventing a new meta-model or format. This choice trades algorithmic novelty for interoperability. By staying within the existing XES standard, any conformant process mining tool can consume ABM logs unmodified. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) requirements derivation from two perspectives, goal-oriented (cost, time, quality, flexibility, following the "Devil's Quadrangle" framework) and system-driven (behavior, semantics, interoperability, drawing on OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions); (2) an event data model instantiating those requirements as a shallow class hierarchy: an abstract XES Event type extended by prompt (user turn) and an abstract AgentEvent type further specialized into agent_start, agent_finish, call_llm, execute_tool, and transfer_to_agent, each carrying attributes such as cost:total, ai:_tokens, duration, ai:response_thought, and ai:error; (3) instantiation on a simulated four-agent O2C system (Order, Inventory, Barista, Customer Service agents built with sequential ReAct-style reasoning) that generated 371 execution traces, converted into the event schema and fed into standard process mining views (directly-follows graphs for discovery, trace-to-specification alignment for conformance checking, cost/latency dashboards for performance analysis, and case clustering for variant analysis). No loss function or learned model is involved; this is a data-modeling and applied-analytics contribution, not a machine learning method. The three critical design decisions are: (a) using opaque per-run event types layered on XES rather than modifying the XES meta-model, justified by tool compatibility, though it constrains expressiveness for concurrent/asynchronous agents (acknowledged in Section 6.4); (b) treating an entire multi-turn conversation as a single case_id, which is administratively convenient but assumes topic continuity, an assumption the authors flag as a real limitation; (c) capturing execution at the framework-interaction level (call_llm, execute_tool) rather than inside a single LLM call, sacrificing visibility into intra-LLM reasoning steps for tractability. Key Results: Descriptive statistics from the 371-case O2C log: 1.2k total agent-orchestration instances, Order Agent processing 348 orders, Inventory Agent checking 249 items, average prompt-to-result time 1m 49s, average LLM-call latency 1.24s, 4,294,572 total tokens (4,088,632 input / 205,940 output) at $9.82 per run, 2.3 agents invoked per case on average. Token attribution showed Barista, Inventory, and Order agents each consuming roughly 1M tokens versus 604k for Customer Service, illustrating uneven cost distribution across agents in the same workflow. Conformance checking surfaced concrete deviations (a calculate_total omission by Order Agent, an estimate_prep_time skip/insertion by Barista Agent) that the authors treat as detected "policy guardrail breaches." In the follow-up practitioner survey (n=18), 78% ranked process-variant visibility as the most valuable insight (vs. 50% for agent performance, 44% for compliance), 83% reported "moderate" to "extreme" transparency improvement, and 72% rated the dashboards "helpful" or "very helpful." There are no baselines: this is a single-condition case study, and no comparison is made against alternative observability tools (e.g., raw LangSmith traces) on the same criteria. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL and, to a lesser degree, NEW FORMULATION (an event-schema extension). The genuinely novel contribution is a concrete, XES-conformant attribute schema for GenAI agent traces plus its practical instantiation and dashboarding on a real MAS; the process mining algorithms applied (directly-follows discovery, alignment-based conformance checking, variant clustering) are standard, off-the-shelf techniques, not new methods. The paper is explicit that it deliberately avoids new algorithms in favor of standard-compliant schema design. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are presented (there is no learned component to ablate), and no statistical significance testing is applicable given the case-study design. The 18-practitioner survey reports counts and percentages from Likert items and thematically coded open responses (with a second-coder consistency check), but sample size is small and there is no control condition or comparison against practitioners using a competing tool. The authors state a public repository (https://github.com/A-rebmann/ABM/) with the O2C implementation, generated event logs, survey instruments, and evaluation dashboards, supporting conceptual reproducibility. A clear methodological caveat, stated by the authors themselves: all agents ran on a single model (gpt-4.1-2025-04-14), so cost, latency, and reasoning-trace availability (which depends on whether a model exposes chain-of-thought) are model-specific findings that may not generalize to other LLM families, including reasoning models like OpenAI o-series or Claude, where ai:response_thought may be unavailable depending on invocation settings. The 371 traces were generated by a single researcher-constructed scenario under laboratory conditions with induced failures, not sampled from production traffic, which the authors flag directly as an external validity threat. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a data model plus a reference implementation and dashboards, evaluated via a case study and practitioner survey). Practical: Yes; the model runs on standard XES-compatible process mining software and a conventional multi-agent LLM stack, requiring no specialized hardware. Efficient: Not quantified relative to alternative observability approaches (e.g., raw AgentOps traces); the paper reports absolute cost/latency of the underlying agent system, not the overhead of instrumentation itself. Effective: The case study shows the model can surface real conformance violations and cost disparities in one constructed scenario, but this is a proxy for governance effectiveness, not a demonstration that ABM reduces actual compliance incidents or resource waste in production. Scalable: Not tested; the authors explicitly flag that concurrent or asynchronous multi-agent interactions beyond a shared session identifier, and scenarios with many interacting business objects, are unaddressed and reserved for future OCED-based extensions. The paper delivers a software artifact (implementation, logs, dashboards, survey instruments in a public repo) rather than a proof technique. Strengths: - Concrete, testable schema design grounded in an explicit seven-requirement derivation (R1-R7) mapped in a traceability table to specific event types and attributes, giving reviewers a verifiable link between stated goals and implementation. - Demonstrates real conformance-checking value on a nontrivial multi-agent system rather than a toy example, surfacing genuine non-deterministic policy deviations (calculate_total omission, prep-time skip) that would be invisible in raw agent framework logs. - The practitioner study, while small, uses a structured four-phase protocol (context setting, simulation, insight elicitation, feedback) with role diversity (strategic, operational, control) and inter-coder review of qualitative themes, which is more rigorous than an informal survey. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly and in detail (Sections 6.3-6.4) that: perceived usefulness was measured, not objective decision accuracy or task outcomes; the workshop setting may introduce novelty/desirability bias; all agents used a single LLM (gpt-4.1) and results may not generalize across model families, especially those with different reasoning-trace availability; the 371 cases came from a single researcher-constructed scenario with induced failures under controlled conditions, not production traffic; the evaluation used sequential ReAct agents only, excluding other MAS architectures; reasoning-level failure analysis depends on ai:response_thought, which some models do not expose; the model treats each call_llm as atomic and cannot see sub-steps within a single LLM invocation; and the case notion assumes topic continuity within a session, unable to dynamically split or merge cases if topics shift mid-conversation. This is an unusually thorough and direct limitations discussion. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The Devil's Quadrangle and requirement-derivation framing is used to justify design choices, but no alternative schema designs are compared, so it is not established that this particular attribute set is necessary or sufficient versus a leaner or differently structured one. The 78%-of-practitioners-favor-variant-analysis finding is reported without acknowledging that participants were shown four specific dashboard views chosen by the authors, which could bias which insight category gets rated most valuable. The paper does not report inter-rater agreement statistics for the thematic coding beyond stating a second researcher reviewed it. There is no comparison of ABM logs against directly using existing AgentOps traces (LangSmith/LangFuse/Phoenix) processed through ad hoc scripts, so the marginal value of standardizing to XES specifically, versus writing a one-off conversion script, is asserted rather than measured. Bottom Line: A BPM or AI-governance practitioner building agent observability for multi-agent LLM deployments gains a concrete, well-justified reference schema and a demonstrated case study showing standard process mining techniques catch real deviations in agent behavior, but should treat the practitioner-utility findings as preliminary perception data from a single-model, single-scenario lab study rather than evidence of governance impact in production.
Search Results 2606.20669 PDF
#69 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Sol Video Inference Engine: Agent-Native Full-Stack Acceleration Framework for Efficient Video Generation

Y Li, J Chen, H Li, H Liu, J Yu, L Zhu, P Luo, S Han… · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Sol Video Inference Engine, an agentic framework that assigns parallel LLM agents to optimize cache, sparse attention, token pruning, quantization, and kernel fusion for a specific (model, hardware, serving configuration) instance, then composes the results into a full acceleration stack under human visual-quality validation. - Why it's important: Video diffusion inference is expensive and the best acceleration recipe does not transfer across models, hardware, or serving configurations, so every new deployment target today requires a separate multi-team manual tuning effort. - How it does it: Independent skill agents search each technique's local configuration space, an integrator agent composes the local candidates into a global stack while resolving cross-technique interactions, and a human validator inspects a fixed set of generations because PSNR-style metrics do not track perceptual video quality. - The good: The framework delivers 2.27x-2.77x end-to-end speedup over an SGLang baseline on three architecturally distinct models (64B MoT Cosmos3-Super, 22B two-stage LTX-2.3, 2B linear-attention SANA-Video) while VBench average scores move by at most -0.54%. - The missing: The paper reports no quantitative measure of agent contribution, iteration count, wall-clock or token cost of the agent search itself, nor a comparison against a human expert or a non-agentic automated tuner (e.g., Bayesian optimization or grid search) on the same search space. - Read it or not: Worth reading for systems engineers building instance-specific inference optimization pipelines or agent-driven performance-engineering harnesses; the acceleration techniques themselves are all prior work, so readers looking for a new caching, sparsity, or quantization method should go to the cited primary sources instead.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile. Practical Impact (55) is the largest single weight and it is capped by the absence of a released code artifact (a GitHub link is referenced but no URL given) and unmeasured agent-search cost. Rigor (40) and Resilience (40) pull the composite down hardest: the central claim that the agentic search is better than a non-agentic autotuner or human expert is never tested, and there are no ablations isolating the agent's contribution from simply applying the known TeaCache/PISA/quantization techniques. Novelty (45) is limited because all five acceleration families are prior work; only the orchestration workflow is new. Weighted: 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.30*55 = 46.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying a video diffusion model at inference time requires choosing a compatible cache policy, sparsity pattern, token-pruning schedule, quantization plan, and kernel-fusion scheme, and the paper's central empirical claim is that the correct choice for each is instance-specific: it depends jointly on model architecture (e.g. a Mixture-of-Transformers versus linear attention), hardware platform (B200 versus RTX 5090), and serving configuration (resolution, video duration, frame rate). Organizations deploying more than one video model across more than one hardware target today re-run this tuning by hand across separate algorithm, kernel, and serving engineering teams, which the paper frames as slow, poorly scaling, and error-prone (Figure 1). The gap being closed is the lack of a systematic, less labor-intensive procedure for navigating this combinatorial (model x hardware x config) space rather than any single new acceleration algorithm. Key Idea: The core move is to decompose full-stack acceleration into a two-level agent hierarchy: per-technique 'skill agents' independently search local configuration spaces (e.g., a cache agent tunes skip-schedule and compensation rule; a quantization agent tunes per-layer bitwidth), and a separate 'integrator agent' performs global composition and search over how these locally-optimal candidates combine, since stacking independently-optimal lossy techniques does not guarantee an acceptable joint result. A human validator closes the loop by supplying visual-quality judgments that automatic metrics like PSNR fail to capture reliably (Figure 6), which the paper uses to justify keeping a human in the loop rather than fully automating quality assessment. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages per deployment instance: (1) parallel skill agents optimize cross-step cache (TeaCache/EasyCache/fixed-step-skip style policies), sparse attention (PISA-style piecewise sparse attention), token pruning (feature-norm saliency-based pruning), quantization (NVFP4/low-bit assignment by layer and timestep), and kernel fusion (GEMM+GELU, GEMM+residual, fused RoPE+norm epilogues) independently, each producing locally optimal candidates; (2) an integrator agent composes candidates into a full stack, resolving interactions such as whether sparse attention remains acceptable when combined with quantization; (3) a human validator inspects a fixed validation set and either accepts the stack or triggers another round targeting more conservative or more aggressive settings. Three design decisions stand out. First, cache policy choice is model-dependent (TeaCache-style for Cosmos3-Super and LTX-2.3, EasyCache for SANA-Video) and justified by measured cache-policy performance per model, which is reasonable. Second, quantization is applied selectively by denoising timestep and layer (e.g., Cosmos3-Super keeps the first and last three denoising steps in high precision) based on the claim that early/late steps are more sensitive; this is plausible but not directly ablated against a uniform-precision baseline in the main text. Third, the choice to keep a human in the loop for final quality acceptance is well-justified by the PSNR-versus-perception mismatch shown in Figure 6, though it means the system is not fully autonomous, which the paper explicitly flags as a limitation. Key Results: On NVIDIA B200 GPUs, end-to-end latency drops from an SGLang baseline of 99.6s to 43.9s (2.27x) for 64B Cosmos3-Super (4-GPU sequence parallelism), from 97.8s to 41.0s (2.38x) for 22B LTX-2.3, and from 29.4s to 10.6s (2.77x) for 2B SANA-Video (Table 1); relative to each model's official (unoptimized) pipeline the speedups are 2.47x, 2.88x, and 3.23x respectively. VBench average scores (Table 2) shift by +0.21%, -0.54%, and -0.21% respectively, all within what the paper calls near-lossless bounds, though per-dimension changes include a -2.09% drop in LTX-2.3's aesthetic quality and a -1.82% drop in its overall consistency score, which the paper does not separately discuss. A single-GPU B200-versus-B300 extension for Cosmos3-Super shows 2.46x and 2.56x speedup respectively (Table 3), attributed to B300's higher NVFP4 throughput. All comparisons are against the paper's own SGLang-based deployment and official baselines rather than against a competing acceleration framework, so there is no head-to-head comparison with, e.g., a manually-tuned expert stack or a different automated tuning method at the same effort budget. Novelty Classification: ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION and, secondarily, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL for the agent orchestration workflow itself. The five acceleration technique families (cross-step cache, sparse attention, token pruning, quantization, kernel fusion) are each drawn from prior published methods (TeaCache, EasyCache, PISA, SVDQuant-style quantization, CUTLASS-epilogue-style fusion); what is new is the agent-native workflow that automates per-technique local search plus cross-technique global composition for a given deployment instance, evaluated across three architecturally distinct models. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports VBench scores across seven dimensions with a baseline-versus-accelerated delta (Table 2), and Appendix A.2 gives detailed per-model hyperparameter settings (cache thresholds, sparsity density, quantization scope) for reproducibility on paper. However, there are no ablations isolating the contribution of the agent search process itself versus a non-agentic or brute-force search over the same technique space, no error bars, seeds, or repeated-run variance reported for either latency or VBench numbers, and no code or model-weight release is mentioned despite a 'Github Code' link appearing in the abstract header (the actual repository URL is not given in the visible text). The technique-family baselines it composes (TeaCache, PISA, SVDQuant-style quantization) are all self-reported and drawn from the same group's or closely related prior work, and quality evaluation is self-administered on VBench with no held-out or third-party quality judge beyond a human validator whose selection criteria and inter-rater agreement are not described. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built deployment framework evaluated by end-to-end serving latency and VBench quality). Practical: Yes, runs on commercially available NVIDIA B200/B300 GPUs and is demonstrated to also work on H200-class and consumer RTX 5090 hardware per Figure 3, though only B200/B300 numbers are reported. Efficient: Delivers 2.27x-2.77x latency reduction over an already-optimized SGLang baseline with no added inference-time compute overhead reported for the agent search phase itself (agent search happens once, offline, per deployment instance). Effective: VBench scores support that quality is preserved within roughly 2% average deviation, though this is a proxy for perceptual quality and the paper itself argues automatic metrics like PSNR are unreliable, creating some tension in relying on VBench as the primary reported quality evidence. Scalable: The paper explicitly frames deployment-instance combinatorics (model x hardware x config) as the central scaling challenge but only demonstrates the framework on three models across two hardware platforms; it does not report agent search wall-clock time or cost as a function of instance count, so scalability of the framework itself to dozens of deployment targets is asserted but not measured. Strengths: - Demonstrates a working agent-orchestrated tuning workflow across three architecturally different backbones (Mixture-of-Transformers, two-stage diffusion, linear attention), which is a genuine test of generalization rather than a single-model demo. - Explicitly documents where automatic quality proxies (PSNR) diverge from human perceptual judgment (Figure 6) and designs the workflow around that finding rather than ignoring it. - Appendix A.2 provides granular per-model configuration detail (exact cache-hit schedules, sparsity density, precision assignment) that would let a practitioner attempt to reproduce the reported operating points. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that the workflow still depends on human judgment for final quality acceptance because automatic similarity metrics are not perceptually aligned, and they identify this dependency as the main obstacle to a more autonomous or scalable system, proposing learned video preference models, artifact detectors, and VLM-based judgment as future work. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No reported cost, latency, or iteration count for the agent search process itself, so the claimed reduction in "manual effort" is qualitative and not benchmarked against, e.g., a human expert's time-to-tune or a non-agentic autotuner under an equal compute/time budget. - No ablation isolating what fraction of the 2x-3x speedup is attributable to the agent-driven search versus simply applying the underlying published techniques (TeaCache, PISA, SVDQuant) with default or lightly-tuned settings. - VBench per-dimension deltas include a -2.09% aesthetic-quality and -1.82% overall-consistency drop for LTX-2.3 that are not discussed despite being the largest per-dimension changes in the paper; this is a dog that doesn't bark given the paper's overall "near-lossless" framing. - All baselines being compared against (SGLang deployment, official pipeline) are the authors' own reference implementations rather than independently verified third-party numbers, and quality evaluation stops at VBench plus an unspecified-size human validation set with no inter-rater reliability reported. Bottom Line: Practitioners building instance-specific inference optimization pipelines for foundation-model serving will find the agent-orchestration pattern (parallel skill search plus integrator composition plus human-in-the-loop validation) directly useful as a systems blueprint, but readers should treat the 2x-3x speedup claims as evidence for the composition of known techniques working well together rather than evidence that the agentic search process itself is more efficient than a manually- or classically-tuned alternative, since that comparison is never made.
Search Results 2606.23743 PDF
#70 — Moderately Innovative (45)

AContext-ENRICHED NL-TO-SQL AGENT FOR PREMIER LEAGUE FOOTBALL DATABASE QUERYING

J Hedlund-Fay · ijcionline.com · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a 400-question NL-to-SQL benchmark for the football-administration domain and shows that a two-stage query-refinement RAG pipeline (RAG-R) outperforms both a monolithic RAG agent (RAG-C) and a ten-shot Chain-of-Thought baseline on exact-match accuracy. - Why it's important: Enterprise NL-to-SQL systems fail silently when correctness depends on external regulatory rules rather than schema structure alone, and real fines (Grimsby Town, £20,000; Liverpool FC, £200,000) show the cost of that failure mode in practice. - How it does it: RAG-R separates domain-knowledge retrieval and query rewriting (via a dedicated LLM call against a vector store of the PL/FA/IFAB rulebooks) from SQL generation, avoiding the context-stuffing that causes RAG-C to hallucinate. - The good: RAG-R improves Exact Set Match by 0.278 over the CoT baseline on the hardest (Difficulty 4) queries, confirmed significant by McNemar's test (chi-squared = 15.6, p < 0.001). - The missing: The 400-question benchmark and its gold SQL were authored by a single domain expert with no cross-validation, and EM/EX metrics only check result-set equality, not logical/semantic equivalence of the generated SQL. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for anyone building RAG pipelines over regulatory or compliance text who wants a concrete illustration of "lost in the middle" degradation in a monolithic tool-use agent versus a decoupled retrieve-then-generate design; skip if you need a validated benchmark or peer-reviewed evaluation, since this is a single-author, single-annotator preprint-grade study in a lower-tier venue.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (38) and Novelty (48) are the two dimensions pulling the composite below 50: the core idea (splitting retrieval/disambiguation from SQL generation) is a direct application of existing query-refinement RAG (RQ-RAG, DIN-SQL) to a new domain with no new method, and the evaluation is single-model (GPT-4o only), single-annotator, single-benchmark, with only one McNemar test on one difficulty tier, no seeds or CIs, and no external NL-to-SQL RAG baseline. Completeness (58) is a relative bright spot given the honest itemized limitations section, but the single-author preprint-grade study with an unreleased benchmark leaves the 0.278 EM gain unverified for generality. Weighted: 0.20*48 + 0.25*38 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*40 = 45.
empirical Novelty 48 Rigor 38 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 58 Resilience 40 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Enterprise NL-to-SQL systems in high-compliance domains must resolve two categories of ambiguity: logical ambiguity (query correctness depends on external, frequently-updated regulatory text, e.g., a 24-hour player registration deadline in the FA Handbook) and linguistic ambiguity (domain jargon like "top goalscorer" that cannot be resolved from schema alone). The English football pyramid is used as a concrete testbed because its governing bodies (Premier League, FA, IFAB) publish external rulebooks whose logic is not encoded in the relational database, and because real compliance failures (fines to Grimsby Town and Liverpool FC for late/ineligible player registrations) demonstrate the cost of this semantic gap in practice. Key Idea: Decoupling domain-knowledge retrieval and query disambiguation from SQL generation, i.e., using one LLM call to rewrite an ambiguous natural-language question into a self-contained, rule-grounded question, then feeding that refined question to a separate SQL-generation LLM call, rather than asking a single agent to jointly retrieve, reason, and generate SQL in one pass. The paper's own framing of this as evidence against "agentic" end-to-end designs in favor of modular pipelines is the paper's central and only real conceptual contribution; the retrieval mechanism (dense FAISS retrieval with e5-large-v2 embeddings) itself is standard RAG. Methodology: The pipeline has four functional stages: (1) benchmark construction, a PostgreSQL database built from public Transfermarkt data (400 clubs, 32,000 players, 148,000 match appearances, 2012-2025), paired with 400 hand-authored (NL, SQL) examples stratified into 4 difficulty tiers, the top tier requiring unstated domain-specific knowledge (DSK); (2) baseline ICL tuning, comparing zero-shot, five-shot (FAISS-retrieved dynamic examples), and ten-shot CoT prompting with gpt-4o-2024-08-06 at temperature 0; (3) RAG-C, a monolithic "agent" architecture where a single LLM call receives the user prompt, dense-retrieved DSK context (chunked from the PL/FA/IFAB handbooks, e5-large-v2 embeddings, fixed-size chunking with 10-token overlap), and produces SQL directly; (4) RAG-R, a two-stage pipeline where an LLM first rewrites the ambiguous prompt into an explicit, rule-grounded prompt (using a hand-engineered instruction template to prevent "instruction drift" toward conversational answering), and a second LLM call then generates SQL from the refined prompt plus the original few-shot/CoT scaffolding. Hyperparameters (chunk size, retrieved-chunk count k, few-shot k) are tuned via grid search on a validation set using Execution Accuracy (EX) and Exact Set Match (EM) as the objective, with faithfulness and relevancy (cosine similarity between synthetic-question and user-query embeddings) as auxiliary retrieval diagnostics. The three design decisions worth flagging: (a) using GPT-4o exclusively with no open-weight or alternative-vendor model, justified only by leaderboard citations rather than a controlled comparison; (b) selecting hyperparameters (e.g., k=5, s=128 for RAG-R) purely by validation-set EM with no reported variance across runs; (c) applying McNemar's test only to the RAG-R vs. CoT comparison on Difficulty 4, not to the RAG-C comparison or to other difficulty tiers, which limits the statistical claims to a single pairwise contrast. Key Results: On the 360-question held-out test set, ten-shot CoT is the strongest ICL-only baseline (0.511 average EM, 0.953 average EX). RAG-C underperforms this baseline (0.493 average EM), while RAG-R exceeds it (0.627 average EM, 0.947 average EX). The largest gap is on Difficulty 4 ("semantic stress test") queries: CoT scores 0.244 EM versus RAG-R's 0.522 EM, a 0.278 absolute improvement confirmed significant by McNemar's test (chi-squared = 15.6, p < 0.001). RAG-R shows a minor regression on Difficulty 1 (simple retrieval) queries relative to five-shot and CoT baselines (0.489 EM vs. 0.567 and 0.633 respectively), which the authors attribute to unnecessary refinement overhead on non-ambiguous queries. All numbers come from a single proprietary benchmark built by the authors themselves; no external benchmark (Spider 2.0, BIRD, Archer) is used for validation, and there is no comparison against any other published RAG-based NL-to-SQL system. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (400-question football-domain NL-to-SQL benchmark requiring external regulatory knowledge) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (modular RAG query-refinement outperforms monolithic RAG agent in a DSK-heavy setting). The core architectural idea, i.e., splitting retrieval/disambiguation from generation, is a direct application of existing query-refinement/decomposition RAG techniques (already cited as RQ-RAG, DIN-SQL decomposition) to a new domain and a new benchmark; no new method or algorithm is proposed. Rigor & Reproducibility: A single statistical test (McNemar's, one comparison) is reported; no confidence intervals, seeds, or repeated runs are given for any of the EM/EX numbers, so the reported deltas (e.g., the 0.278 Difficulty 4 gain) rest on a single evaluation pass with T=0.0. No ablation isolates which part of the RAG-R refinement prompt (the rule retrieval vs. the anti-drift instructions) drives the gain. The benchmark, database schema (Appendix A, referenced but not included in the reviewed pages), and code are not stated as publicly released beyond the DDL description; the underlying Transfermarkt data is public (Kaggle), which supports schema reproducibility, but the authored NL/SQL pairs and prompts are not confirmed as released. The benchmark was built and evaluated by the same team with a single domain-expert annotator, and tuning/selection (chunk size, k, few-shot count) was done on the same validation split later used to report test-set superiority, which is standard practice but leaves the Difficulty-1 regression and the choice of k=5/s=128 for RAG-R unexplained beyond post-hoc rationalization. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes, it runs on a standard PostgreSQL instance with API calls to GPT-4o; no specialized hardware is required. Efficient: RAG-R adds a second LLM call and a dense-retrieval step over CoT alone (added latency reported in Table 3, up to 7.94s per query for one configuration), a nontrivial per-query cost increase not benchmarked against RAG-C's or CoT's latency in the main results table. Effective: The evidence (EM gains on Difficulty 4) shows it improves exact-match accuracy on the authors' own benchmark, but EM measures result-set equality, not verified logical correctness, so "solves the stated problem" is only partially demonstrated. Scalable: Not tested; the benchmark uses 400 clubs and 148,000 appearances, but no experiment varies database size, rule-corpus size, or query volume, so scaling behavior at 10x the current corpus is unevaluated and unclaimed. Strengths: - Constructs a benchmark grounded in a real, cited compliance failure mode (Grimsby Town and Liverpool FC fines), giving the difficulty-tier design (especially Tier 4) a concrete real-world referent rather than an arbitrary synthetic split. - Provides a qualitative case study (Section 5.4, Figs. 6) that traces exactly how RAG-C's monolithic agent gets "distracted" attempting to recompute homegrown-player status from first principles versus RAG-R's refined prompt injecting the regulatory constant directly, a concrete mechanistic explanation for the quantitative gap rather than just a number. - Applies McNemar's test rather than reporting only raw EM deltas for the paper's central claim, giving at least one statistically grounded comparison. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) the benchmark relies on a single expert annotator with no cross-validation; (2) EM/EX do not verify semantic/logical equivalence of generated SQL, only result-set match; (3) RAG-R shows minor performance degradation on low-complexity queries, motivating a proposed adaptive-retrieval/confidence-scoring extension; (4) the RAG-C failure points to an unresolved research gap on primacy/recency bias mitigation via prompt reordering. This is a direct, itemized limitations section rather than a minimized aside. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison to any other published NL-to-SQL RAG system (e.g., RQ-RAG, DIN-SQL variants, MAC-SQL) is run on this benchmark; the entire empirical case rests on the authors' own baselines and their own two architectures. - Only one LLM (GPT-4o) is evaluated; the claim that RAG-R's advantage stems from architectural decoupling rather than an idiosyncrasy of this specific model's attention/context handling is not tested against a second model family. - The refinement prompt (Fig. 5) was hand-engineered specifically to fix an observed "instruction drift" failure mode discovered during development on this same benchmark, raising a mild Goodhart's Law concern: the RAG-R architecture may be tuned to this benchmark's particular phrasing patterns rather than reflecting a general property of query-refinement pipelines. - The paper reports validation-set hyperparameter selection and test-set results but does not report how much the validation and test splits overlap in query phrasing or rule coverage, so the degree of held-out independence is unclear. - Single-author work (undergraduate/master's-style acknowledgments to a dissertation supervisor) in a journal (IJCI) with no indication of a standard peer-reviewed venue's review rigor; claims should be read as preliminary. Bottom Line: The RAG-R-vs-RAG-C contrast is a useful, concretely illustrated data point on modular versus monolithic RAG design for regulation-grounded NL-to-SQL, but the single-model, single-annotator, single-benchmark, no-external-baseline evaluation means the generality of the 0.278 EM gain is unverified, and a practitioner would learn more from the qualitative failure-mode case study (Section 5.4) than from treating the quantitative benchmark numbers as established results.
Search Results
#71 — Moderately Innovative (45)

LLM-KT: Enhancing Large Language Models with Knowledge Tracing via Multi-Level Plug-and-Play Alignment

Z Wang, J Zhou, Q Chen, B Jiang, Q Bai, L Dou, L He · aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLM-KT, a framework that injects traditional knowledge-tracing (KT) sequence-model embeddings and compressed textual-history embeddings into an LLM via soft-prompted placeholder tokens, trained with LoRA. - Why it's important: Knowledge tracing predicts whether a student will answer the next question correctly, and existing LLM adaptations either lose ID-based sequential signal or exceed context windows when fed raw interaction history. - How it does it: Two plug-in projector modules (a Semantic History Projector encoding question/concept text and a Behavioral Dynamics Projector encoding DKT/AKT-derived sequence embeddings) each map their modality into the LLM's embedding space, and their fused output physically replaces specific placeholder token embeddings in a natural-language prompt. - The good: LLM-KT reaches 0.8884 average AUC across four benchmarks, beating the strongest baseline (LLM-FT_ID at 0.8554 AUC) by 3.3 points and beating direct GPT-4o prompting by roughly 10 AUC points on Assist2009. - The missing: No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere, so a 0.3-1.9 point AUC margin over the next-best baseline (e.g., 0.8291 vs 0.8103 on Nips2020) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run noise. - Read it or not: Worth reading for KT practitioners evaluating whether to bolt an LLM onto an existing sequence model rather than replace it, but the gains over strong non-LLM baselines (DKT/AKT: 0.795-0.902 AUC) are modest relative to the 4-16x training/inference cost increase (Table 12), so cost-sensitive deployments should look elsewhere.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Practical Impact (35) and Non-Obviousness (40) pull the composite down: soft-prompt embedding injection is a fairly obvious recombination, the method ships no code, and the 3.3-point AUC gain comes at 4x training and up to 97x inference cost. Rigor (48) is limited by no seeds, no significance tests, and no cross-dataset transfer despite a portability claim, so 0.3-1.9 point margins over the next-best baseline cannot be distinguished from noise. The bolt-an-LLM-onto-an-existing-model framing partly resembles the negative calibration set. Weighted composite: 45.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 48 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing infers a student's mastery state from a sequence of past question-answer interactions to predict future performance, used by adaptive tutoring systems to plan instruction. Traditional sequence models (DKT, AKT, SAKT) treat questions and concepts as opaque integer IDs and so cannot exploit semantic relationships between questions (e.g., that one problem is a prerequisite for another), while pre-trained language models (BERT-based KT variants) encode question text but lack the reasoning capacity and world knowledge of modern LLMs. Directly repurposing LLMs for KT hits two obstacles: LLMs interpret IDs as ungrounded token sequences with no notion of sequential interaction dynamics (a splitting-into-multiple-tokens problem for numeric IDs), and full interaction histories (200+ questions, ~77 tokens each) exceed practical context budgets, with the paper reporting that naive LLaMA fine-tuning and GPT-4o prompting underperform lightweight DL baselines as a result. Key Idea: Rather than asking the LLM to learn sequential and ID-semantic structure from scratch via fine-tuning or raw-text prompting, the paper freezes a traditional KT sequence encoder (DKT/AKT) and a text encoder (LLaMA2/BERT/MPNet), trains lightweight MLP "adapter" projectors to map each encoder's output into the target LLM's embedding space, and substitutes those projected vectors directly into the input embedding positions of specific placeholder tokens (e.g., [QuesEmbed], [ConcEmbed]) in an otherwise natural-language prompt. This is a soft-prompting/embedding-injection idea applied to a genuinely new modality pairing (sequential ID embeddings + compressed text embeddings), not a new representation-learning method in itself. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages per question/concept attribute k in {q, c}: (1) a Context Encoder (frozen LLaMA2, BERT, or MPNet) produces text embedding r_k^ctx from raw question/concept text; a Context Adapter (MLP) projects this into the LLM's embedding dimension d^e, giving h_k^ctx (Semantic History Projector). (2) A Sequence Encoder (frozen DKT or AKT) produces a behavioral embedding r_k^seq from the raw ID sequence; a Sequence Adapter (MLP) projects this to h_k^seq (Behavioral Dynamics Projector). (3) A gated fusion function G (paper uses simple addition after an ablation in Table 5 shows addition, averaging, and concatenation perform similarly, within 0.3 AUC points) combines h_k^ctx and h_k^seq into a single embedding e_k, which physically replaces the placeholder token's embedding in the LLM's input sequence. The whole system is trained end-to-end as conditional generation (predicting the token 'Yes'/'No'), with LoRA (rank 32, alpha 32) adapting the frozen LLaMA2-7B backbone; the base LLM, context encoder, and sequence encoder are all frozen, so only the adapters (51.4M params) and LoRA weights (91.4M total trainable) are updated. Three design decisions stand out: freezing the sequence/context encoders is well-justified by the ablation (Table 2, removing either component costs 0.3-3.1 AUC points) but the paper never tests whether jointly fine-tuning them would help; using addition for fusion is empirically justified via Table 5 but the mechanism for gating is never specified beyond the label 'gated fusion' in the text, an inconsistency with the actual add-based implementation; and the choice of LLaMA2-7B backbone is validated for transferability by an appendix swap to Mistral-7B (0.8843 vs 0.8884 average AUC), a reasonable but single-alternative check. Key Results: On four benchmarks (Assist2009, Assist2015, Junyi, Nips2020), LLM-KT achieves average AUC 0.8884 / ACC 0.8302, versus the best baseline LLM-FT_ID at 0.8554 AUC (a 3.3-point margin) and the best DL-based baseline (UKT/LPKT, ~0.7955-0.7956 average AUC not directly listed but per-dataset UKT reaches 0.8563 on Assist2009). Against recent LLM-based competitors CLST (0.8842 AUC on Junyi) and DDKT (0.8103 AUC on Nips2020), LLM-KT leads by 1.8 and 1.9 points respectively on those datasets. Directly prompting GPT-4o with full text history yields only 0.7274 AUC on Assist2009, underperforming most DL baselines, supporting the paper's claim that raw-context prompting fails at this scale (200+ interactions per record). Sequence-length ablation (Figure 3-4) shows LLM-KT's AUC improves by ~10 points from length 20 to 100 on Assist2015 while DKT/AKT baselines plateau or decline, though on Junyi (short-term-focused dataset) LLM-KT's performance also declines with longer sequences, an admitted limitation. All experiments are standard-scale KT benchmarks (4K-72K students, 100-16K questions), not stress-scale. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the dual-projector soft-prompt injection architecture for fusing frozen sequence-model and text-model representations into an LLM) applied within an established problem (KT) using an established technique family (parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning, soft prompting). The genuinely new element is the multi-level (task + modality) alignment design and the specific token-substitution mechanism for heterogeneous ID/text/sequence data; the LoRA fine-tuning, MLP adapters, and frozen encoder-adapter pattern are standard techniques recombined for this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and useful: removing question/concept data modalities (Table 2) and removing either projector module both degrade AUC by 0.3-3.1 points, supporting the claim that both components matter. Context-encoder and sequence-encoder choice ablations (Tables 3-4) and a fusion-function ablation (Table 5) are also included. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, multiple random seeds, or statistical significance tests appear anywhere in the paper, so the reported 0.3-3.3 point AUC margins over strong baselines cannot be assessed for robustness. No code, data, or model weight release URL is mentioned anywhere in the paper or appendix. Baselines for DL-based, PLM-based, and context-aware methods are taken from original papers or re-implementation papers rather than the authors' own re-runs, which is standard practice but means the comparison is not conducted under identical splits/seeds across all methods; the paper does divide its own experiments in an 8:1:1 split following MRT-KT. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. Practical: Yes, runs on a single NVIDIA A800 GPU as reported, though A800s are not commodity hardware and no CPU or consumer-GPU feasibility is discussed. Efficient: Substantially more expensive than lightweight alternatives: 2706 s/epoch training and 350 ms/sample inference versus DKT's 674 s/16.6 ms and AKT's 172 s/3.6 ms (Table 12), a roughly 4x training and 21-97x inference latency increase for a 3.3-point AUC gain over the next-best method. Effective: The evidence (AUC/ACC across four standard benchmarks, plus ablations) shows the method solves the stated correctness-prediction task directly, not merely a proxy, though the underlying label distribution/class balance per dataset is not reported alongside AUC/ACC. Scalable: Not tested; the paper explicitly lists "handling noise in extremely long sequences" as future work and admits performance can decline with sequence length on certain datasets (Junyi), so scalability to 10x-100x history length is an acknowledged open problem rather than a demonstrated capability. The work delivers no released software artifact (no code/data/weights link found); it stops at paper form with an architecture description and prompt templates in the appendix. Strengths: - The four-dataset, four-baseline-category comparison (DL, PLM, context-aware, LLM-based) is broad relative to typical KT papers and includes recent 2025 LLM-based competitors (CLST, DDKT), giving a credible picture of where the field stood at submission time. - The ablation in Table 2 isolates the incremental contribution of each modality (question ID/text vs concept ID/text) and each projector module, which is a genuine diagnostic rather than a single combined result. - The backbone-swap experiment (LLaMA2-7B to Mistral-7B, Appendix D) is a reasonable, if minimal, generalizability check that most KT papers proposing LLM integration skip entirely. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly (Section 6) that the hybrid architecture incurs higher computational cost and inference latency than lightweight traditional methods, that performance depends on availability of high-quality textual data, and that handling noise in extremely long sequences and generalizing to unseen questions remain open problems. This is a direct, non-minimized acknowledgment of the method's practical cost and scope limits. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No statistical significance testing or multiple-seed reporting anywhere, so AUC gaps as small as 0.3-0.7 points (e.g., LLM-KT vs LLM-FT_ID on Nips2020: 0.8291 vs not directly comparable, but DDKT vs LLM-KT gap of 1.9 points) cannot be distinguished from noise. - The text describes the fusion function G as "gated fusion" in the method section, but the actual implementation and ablation (Table 5) use simple addition, average, or concatenation, none of which is a gating mechanism; this terminology inconsistency is never resolved. - The paper claims "generalizability" and "portability to new domains" as a core motivation for using LLMs (Section 1), but no cross-dataset transfer experiment (train on one KT dataset, test on another) is run to support this; all evaluation is within-dataset train/test splits. - Nips2020's question text was manually converted from figures (stated in Appendix B), introducing a manual, unaudited data-curation step whose error rate is not characterized. - No comparison of wall-clock training cost normalized by the marginal AUC gain is offered as a first-class result; the cost analysis (Table 12, Appendix E) is relegated to an appendix and is not discussed in the main-text conclusions despite being material to whether the 3.3-point gain justifies a 4-97x compute cost increase. Bottom Line: A practitioner already running a DL-based KT pipeline (DKT/AKT) gains a documented recipe for grafting LLM semantic reasoning onto it via frozen-encoder-plus-adapter injection, but the 0.3-3.3 point AUC improvement over strong non-LLM and LLM-FT baselines, absent significance testing, does not clearly justify the reported 4x training-time and up to 97x inference-latency cost for most production KT deployments.
Search Results
#72 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Decoupling Generalization and Adaptation in Meta-Learning for Large Language Models

N Vetcha, B Xu, D Liu · aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DeGAML-LLM, a meta-learning framework that splits LLM adaptation into a frozen task-conditioned LoRA parameter generator and a separately-trained closed-loop RL policy that refines those parameters without backpropagating into the generator. - Why it's important: Coupled meta-learning methods (MAML-en-LLM, ABMLL) tie generalization and adaptation together through a shared gradient path, forcing every adaptation strategy to be gradient-based and fixed at meta-training time, which limits flexibility for heterogeneous downstream tasks. - How it does it: Stage 1 trains a convolutional parameter generator via MSE regression on collected LoRA checkpoints (no adaptation dynamics encoded); Stage 2 freezes the generator and trains an RL policy (ReST-EM) to choose among four adaptation strategies (test-time training, LoRA subspace mixing, latent-space steering, test-time scaling/ensembling) using validation-based binary rewards. - The good: On Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct, decoupled adaptation adds +27.8 points out-of-domain and +8.3 in-domain over the generator-alone ablation, and the paper reports consistent gains over ABMLL and MAML-en-LLM baselines at two model scales (Table 1). - The missing: All results come from a single seed ("seed 999" stated explicitly in Appendix C) with no error bars, variance across seeds, or significance testing, so the reported deltas (some as small as +0.1-+1.0 points) cannot be distinguished from noise. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for anyone building LLM meta-learning or test-time adaptation pipelines who wants a menu of RL-selectable adaptation strategies, but the lack of seed variance and the small-model-only evaluation (0.5B/1.5B) mean the headline numbers should not be trusted at face value.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (38) is the decisive weakness: all results come from a single seed (seed 999) with no variance reporting despite inter-method deltas under 1 point, the promised code has no URL, and baselines differ in both architecture and compute so the decoupling claim is confounded (no compute-matched comparison). Impact (38) and Resilience (42) are limited by evaluation only at 0.5B/1.5B, well below the 7B+ scale the paper's own motivation invokes. The decoupled generator-plus-RL-policy idea is a reasonable NEW METHOD (Novelty 55), but the evidence is suggestive rather than conclusive. Weighted composite: 45.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 38 Non-Obv 52 Completeness 45 Resilience 42 Impact 38
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs per downstream task is expensive even with parameter-efficient methods like LoRA. Meta-learning approaches such as MAML-en-LLM and ABMLL try to learn a shared initialization that adapts quickly to new tasks with few examples, but both couple the objective of learning a generalizable initialization with the objective of enabling fast task-specific adaptation inside a single bi-level optimization. This coupling forces adaptation to be gradient-based (or Bayesian-inference-based) and tied to whatever trajectory was used during meta-training, which the paper argues is overly restrictive when task discrepancies are large, metrics are non-differentiable, or feedback is otherwise heterogeneous. Key Idea: Detach the adaptation mechanism from the meta-training gradient path entirely: train a parameter generator to only predict good task-conditioned LoRA initializations via a supervised MSE checkpoint-reconstruction loss, then train a separate RL policy on top of the frozen generator's outputs to pick and configure an adaptation strategy (TTT, subspace mixing, latent-space steering, or ensemble/test-time scaling) per task at test time. Because the RL policy's gradients never reach the generator, the two objectives cannot fight each other, and the choice of adaptation mechanism becomes a discrete action the policy can adapt per task rather than something baked in during meta-training. Methodology: Stage 1 (offline): for each meta-training task, LoRA adapters are pretrained for 75 steps (lr 1e-4, batch 32, up to 5000 samples) then fine-tuned for 50 more steps (lr 1e-5), yielding ~125 checkpoints per task (~5000 training pairs of prompt-batch embeddings and checkpoint parameters per task). LoRA weights are layer-split, normalized, and tokenized into fixed-size chunks with 2D positional embeddings; a 3-branch (width/height/layer) 2D-convolutional decoder (inspired by prior parameter-generation work) is trained via MSE between generated and ground-truth checkpoint parameters (Eq. 5/10), using Sentence-BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) task embeddings as input. Stage 2 (online): the generator is frozen and detached; the RL policy pi_psi observes validation-performance state signals and samples discrete adaptation actions per task, executing T adaptation steps, then is updated via ReST-EM policy gradients on binary rewards (whether adapted parameters beat initialization). Two design decisions stand out: (1) the binary (not continuous) reward signal is a coarse training signal for a policy meant to select among fairly different strategy families, and its effect on optimization stability is not analyzed; (2) the generator's checkpoint-collection procedure uses only 5000 training samples per task for 125 steps, which is a small optimization budget whose adequacy for capturing "cross-task parameter manifolds" is asserted rather than validated against alternative budgets. Key Results: On Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct in-domain (ARC-c/e, HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, WinoGrande), DeGAML-LLM averages 67.7 vs. MAML-en-LLM 65.5, ABMLL 62.4, No-Meta-Train-LoRA 64.0, and Union-Train-LoRA 58.3. On the smaller Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct, DeGAML-LLM averages 58.4 vs. MAML-en-LLM 51.9, ABMLL 42.1 — a larger relative gain at the smaller scale. Out-of-domain (GSM-8K, MATH, DivLogicEval, SocialIQA, CodeMMLU, JAMA Clinical), DeGAML-LLM averages 47.5 (1.5B) and 34.2 (0.5B) versus the next-best baseline (MAML-en-LLM) at 42.3 and 26.4 respectively. Ablations (Table 2) show the generator alone (no RL) already beats the frozen base model by +22.9 out-of-domain points (0.5B), and adding RL adaptation contributes a further +27.8 out-of-domain (0.5B) / +12.6 (1.5B). All experiments use only two small-to-mid model scales (0.5B, 1.5B) and 20 adaptation datapoints per target task (following the ABMLL protocol); no results are shown at 7B+ scale where LoRA-based meta-learning is more commonly deployed in practice. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the decoupled generator + RL-adaptation-policy framework) and, to a lesser extent, NEW FORMULATION (recasting meta-learning's bi-level objective as two independently-optimized stages). The generator architecture (2D-convolutional parameter tokenization/decoding) is adapted directly from prior parameter-generation work (cited as Wang et al., 2025 and Liang et al., 2025), and the four adaptation strategies (TTT, LoRA mixing, latent steering, test-time ensembling) are each individually known techniques; the genuine novelty is using RL to select among them per-task without gradient coupling to the generator, rather than any single component being new.
